- Results service: `bench_results.request` + Redis cache hit/miss.

Logan Wright: can build a single dashboard with 3 sections: (1) end-to-end (CDN), (2) origin (marketing web), (3) results service/cache.

Tyler Benson: +1. Also can we add a simple “launch health” tile for Marketing ops? Something like p95 TTFB + 5xx rate + cache hit %. I can link it in the launch war-room channel.

Sean Gallagher: cool. Proposal for alerting (Datadog monitors -> PagerDuty SRE oncall):
1) CDN edge 5xx for benchmark paths
2) Origin 5xx for benchmark paths
3) CDN edge TTFB p95 elevated
4) Results JSON endpoint latency p95 elevated
5) Cache hit ratio drops (miss storm indicator)

Sean Gallagher: @Logan Wright can you draft the exact queries? I’ll wire PagerDuty routing.

Datadog Bot: Sean Gallagher created dashboard “Benchmark Launch — Web + CDN Health”

Logan Wright: draft monitors (all filtered to benchmark traffic only):

Logan Wright: 1) CDN edge 5xx rate (Cloudflare)
Query:
`(sum:cf.edge.requests{path:/benchmarks*,env:prod}.as_count() - sum:cf.edge.requests{path:/benchmarks*,env:prod,status:2xx}.as_count() - sum:cf.edge.requests{path:/benchmarks*,env:prod,status:3xx}.as_count() - sum:cf.edge.requests{path:/benchmarks*,env:prod,status:4xx}.as_count()) / sum:cf.edge.requests{path:/benchmarks*,env:prod}.as_count()`
Threshold: > 0.5% for 5m (warn), > 1% for 5m (alert)

Logan Wright: 2) CDN error responses specifically 524/522 (origin timeout)
Query:
`sum:cf.edge.requests{path:/benchmarks*,env:prod,status:522}.as_count() + sum:cf.edge.requests{path:/benchmarks*,env:prod,status:524}.as_count()`
Threshold: > 20 in 5m (warn), > 50 in 5m (alert)

Alicia Perez: for the landing page, can we also watch Core Web Vitals? Not sure we have that fully instrumented but at least RUM LCP/TTFB would be great.

Logan Wright: we have RUM for the marketing site. Adding to dashboard (not paging):
- RUM: `view.loading_time` p75 for view `benchmarks-latency-per-dollar`
- RUM: `resource.duration` for `latest.json` fetch

Logan Wright: 3) CDN edge TTFB p95
Query:
`p95:cf.edge.ttfb_ms{path:/benchmarks*,env:prod}`
Threshold: > 1200ms for 10m (warn), > 2000ms for 10m (alert)

Logan Wright: 4) Results JSON endpoint latency p95 (APM)
Query:
`p95:bench_results.request.duration{route:GET_/api/benchmarks/v1/public/latest.json,env:prod}`
Threshold: > 600ms for 10m (warn), > 1000ms for 10m (alert)

Logan Wright: 5) Cache hit ratio
We emit `bench_results.cache.hit` and `.miss` counters.
Query:
`sum:bench_results.cache.hit{env:prod}.as_count() / (sum:bench_results.cache.hit{env:prod}.as_count() + sum:bench_results.cache.miss{env:prod}.as_count())`
Threshold: < 0.90 for 5m (warn), < 0.80 for 5m (alert)

Neha Kapoor: thresholds look reasonable. For paging: I’d page on (a) elevated 5xx OR (b) sustained p95 TTFB > 2s OR (c) cache hit < 80%. Warns can stay Slack-only.

Sean Gallagher: +1. We should also catch “silent badness” where we’re 200s but super slow. So TTFB alert paging seems fine.

Harish Reddy: any chance we’ll see 429s from WAF/rate limit during spikes? That’ll look like “broken” to users.

Felix Schneider: if we start rate limiting benchmark JSON too aggressively, the table will fail. Can we monitor 429 separately so we can tune quickly?

Logan Wright: yep add non-paging monitor for 429 rate:
`sum:cf.edge.requests{path:/benchmarks*,env:prod,status:429}.as_count()`
and for the JSON endpoint specifically:
`sum:cf.edge.requests{path:/api/benchmarks/v1/public/latest.json,env:prod,status:429}.as_count()`

Sean Gallagher: 429 monitor should notify eng-sre + eng-platform (not PD) unless it’s huge.

PagerDuty: Sean Gallagher updated service “SRE On-Call” routing rule: source=Datadog monitors tagged `benchmark_launch:true`

Neha Kapoor: also please add a synthetic check from 3 geos (us-east, eu-west, apac) that hits:
- GET landing page
- GET latest.json
and validates HTTP 200 + body size > some minimum (avoid empty responses).

Logan Wright: can do. We can assert `latest.json` body contains `schema_version` string.

Sean Gallagher: nice. @Felix Schneider what key should we check? `schema_version` exists?

Felix Schneider: yep `"schema_version": "1."` should be present. Also `generated_at`.

Logan Wright: adding synthetics:
- “Benchmark landing page (HTML)”
- “Benchmark results JSON (public)”
Locations: AWS N. Virginia, Frankfurt, Singapore
Run every 1 min, alert on 2 consecutive failures.

Alicia Perez: can we get a “CDN cache status breakdown” chart? If cache starts missing, we’ll want to see it quickly.

Logan Wright: added widget: stack of cache statuses for `path:/api/benchmarks/v1/public/*` -> HIT/MISS/BYPASS.

Sean Gallagher: cool. Last bit: comms.
- Dashboard link goes to #marketing + #announcements?
- Paging goes to SRE oncall only.

Tyler Benson: I’ll post dashboard link in launch war-room (#marketing) + add to run-of-show doc. Please also drop quick “how to read” notes (what’s normal vs bad) so non-eng folks don’t panic.

Neha Kapoor: +1. Minimal legend:
- green: p95 TTFB < 1.2s
- yellow: 1.2–2.0s
- red: >2.0s or 5xx >1%

Sean Gallagher: action items:
- @Logan Wright finalize monitors + synthetics, tag `benchmark_launch:true`
- @Sean Gallagher connect monitors to PD, add Slack notif to #eng-sre
- @Tyler Benson share dashboard link + legend with Marketing

Datadog Bot: Logan Wright created monitor “Benchmark Launch: CDN 5xx rate” (tags: benchmark_launch:true)
Datadog Bot: Logan Wright created monitor “Benchmark Launch: CDN p95 TTFB” (tags: benchmark_launch:true)
Datadog Bot: Logan Wright created monitor “Benchmark Launch: Results JSON p95 latency” (tags: benchmark_launch:true)
Datadog Bot: Logan Wright created monitor “Benchmark Launch: Cache hit ratio low” (tags: benchmark_launch:true)
Datadog Bot: Logan Wright created synthetic test “Benchmark results JSON (public)”
Datadog Bot: Logan Wright created synthetic test “Benchmark landing page (HTML)”

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: thanks all. Let’s do a quick dry run tomorrow morning — we can temporarily drop thresholds to confirm PD pages correctly, then revert.

Neha Kapoor: please do it outside of peak. And add a note in the oncall handoff: if cache hit drops, first move is to increase CDN TTL / prewarm, not to scale origin blindly.

Felix Schneider: I can add a manual prewarm job (curl latest.json from a few POPs) if we want, but hopefully the cache holds.

Sean Gallagher: let’s keep prewarm as fallback. For now, dashboards + alerts are the priority. Closing loop tomorrow.

title: #eng-sre: 1748704420
preview:
Rafael Mendes: putting up for review: proposed paging thresholds for max-bound saturation + queue buildup for Dedicated autoscaling bounds. goal is catch "we're pinned at max and customers are queuing" without paging on normal bursty stuff.

Rafael Mendes: draft alert rules (names tbd):
1) WARN: max_bound_hit_rate > 0.10 for 15m
2) PAGE: max_bound_hit_rate > 0.25 for 10m AND p95_request_latency_ms > SLO_p95 for 10m
3) PAGE: max_bound_hit_rate > 0.25 for 10m AND queue_depth_p95 > 0 for 10m (if latency SLO not wired for that deployment)

Rafael Mendes: scope: per deployment_id + region. routed to Dedicated oncall (platform-infra) during pilot, then SRE primary after GA.

Aisha Bello: +1 on needing the "AND". if we page on bound_hit alone we'll get hammered any time someone sets max low intentionally.

Miles Thornton: agree. also, bound_hit_rate is new — do we have confidence it isn't going to be super noisy during the first week while folks tune cooldown/dampening?

Vanessa Ortiz: bound_hit_rate is emitted per autoscaler loop tick (every 15s). it’s basically "clamped_by_max" / total decisions. it will spike if desired jitters around max.

Vanessa Ortiz: we added cooldown + dampening in the loop, but early pilot did show some oscillation if predictor overshot. (that should be better post PR-48288 + the null predictor hotfix)

Allison Grant: from observability side: per deployment+region is okay cardinality-wise (Dedicated counts are low), but we should avoid labels like model_id or customer_name. please keep it to account_id, deployment_id, region.

Sergio Costa: on the dashboard spec we already have panels for desired vs bounded + queue depth. alert could link to drilldown panel. makes triage faster.

Miles Thornton: question: why 0.25 for 10m? what’s the expected baseline when max is correctly set but we hit occasional peaks?

Logan Wright: we replayed a couple traces in sim: "healthy" max-bound hits were usually <5% and very spiky. sustained >20% correlated with either (a) max set too low for ramp or (b) region shortage preventing scale, which also shows up in provisioning_failures.

Aisha Bello: i’d prefer PAGE be tied to user impact signal (latency or queue). if queue depth metric is flaky for some models (batching makes it weird), latency should be the primary.

Keiko Yamamoto: queue depth can be misleading when batching is high; it may sit >0 even when throughput is fine. we should use "queue_wait_ms_p95" if we have it instead of raw depth.

Allison Grant: we do have queue_wait_ms (from fleet gateway). it’s p95 over 1m windows, label set matches. recommend we alert on queue_wait_ms_p95 > 2000ms or something.

Rafael Mendes: good call. swapping queue_depth for queue_wait_ms. what threshold feels right? dedicated SLO is p95 end-to-end latency; queue wait is a component.

Aisha Bello: suggest queue_wait_ms_p95 > 1500ms for 10m as impact proxy. that’s big enough to matter but not so sensitive it pages on minor blips.

Miles Thornton: also need to consider customers with intentionally "queueing" behavior (they’ll accept slower during peak to stay within cap). do we want paging for that if it’s expected?

Rafael Mendes: that’s the tricky part. we don’t want to wake up on "expected cap". but we do want to know when customer is unknowingly pinned and breaching their own SLA.

Vanessa Ortiz: could we gate paging on a new config flag? like "customer_slo_enforced" or "autoscaling_bounds_contractual"? not sure it exists.

Rafael Mendes: not in v1. we have policy fields but not "page me" knobs.

Sergio Costa: maybe severity split:
- WARN when max bound hit sustained (so CS can see it / daily review)
- PAGE only when bound hit + latency SLO breach (explicit impact)

Aisha Bello: +1. WARNs can go to a Slack channel / ticketing, not PD.

Miles Thornton: +1. and for pilots we can be a bit more aggressive on WARN to learn.

Allison Grant: note: latency SLO label availability is inconsistent per deployment. some enterprise customers only ship app-level latency, not gateway-level.

Logan Wright: control plane has per deployment SLO target, but metrics pipeline might not have it. we can still alert on generic p95_request_latency_ms > 2x baseline maybe? (but that becomes hairy)

Rafael Mendes: let’s keep it simple for now:
- PAGE: max_bound_hit_rate > 0.25 for 10m AND (p95_request_latency_ms > slo_p95 for 10m OR queue_wait_ms_p95 > 1500 for 10m)
- WARN: max_bound_hit_rate > 0.10 for 15m (no impact requirement)

Keiko Yamamoto: pls add a third condition to avoid paging during known region shortages: if provisioning_error_rate is high, it’s not an autoscaler bug, it’s capacity. still customer impact though.

Miles Thornton: even if it’s capacity, we probably still want page? impact is impact. but the runbook should branch: "pinned due to max" vs "min unmet / capacity shortage".

Rafael Mendes: agree. page either way, but alert annotations should include "clamp_reason=max" vs "capacity_unavailable". we have clamp_reason label now right?

Vanessa Ortiz: for max-bound, yes. for min unmet due to shortage, separate metric (min_unmet_seconds_total). different alert.

Aisha Bello: can we add "for" to page = 15m instead of 10m? to reduce wakeups on transient ramps.

Rafael Mendes: if it’s a real ramp, 15m might be too slow (customers feel it). but with impact condition maybe 10m is ok.

Sergio Costa: what about requiring 2 consecutive evaluation windows (Grafana) => effectively 10m already. i can configure eval every 1m, for=10m.

Allison Grant: also watch out for aggregation. make sure bound_hit_rate is computed as a rate over decisions, not a gauge from last tick. otherwise prometheus will do weird stuff.

Logan Wright: it’s a counter pair (bound_hit_total, decision_total). recording rule computes ratio over 5m. so alert should use that recording rule.

Rafael Mendes: cool. action items:
- Sergio/Allison: update alert rule to use ratio recording rule + queue_wait_ms, and route WARN to #dedicated-scaling-alerts (no PD)
- Aisha/Miles: sanity check thresholds after first pilot week and adjust
- Keiko/Vanessa: ensure runbook has "customer intentionally capped" note + comms guidance

PagerDuty: (system) Rafael Mendes updated routing rules for service "Dedicated Autoscaling" (pilot) — WARN notifications to Slack, PAGE notifications to PD.

Miles Thornton: :thumbsup: please add to runbook: when paging, first check if bounded_desired == max AND saturation/latency. if yes: coordinate w/ CS to ask customer if max cap is intentional before scaling override.

Aisha Bello: +1. and we should explicitly say: do not raise max without customer approval unless it’s an incident and we have pre-approved emergency spend.

Rafael Mendes: yep, adding that. thanks all.

title: #eng-sre: 1747805432
preview:
Ava Chen: Heads-up — saw three p95 GPU-util spikes in us-west this morning that paged oncall. All recovered within 30–90s. We should adjust paging vs ticketing to avoid wakeups for these micro-flares. Thoughts?

Ravi Patel: +1. Do we know cardinality? per-pod or cluster-wide?

Maya Ortiz: Cluster-level spikes, not single pod. Prometheus shows short-lived 2–3x p95 bumps correlated with scheduling churn.

Luca Marino: Agree noise. Proposal: keep page for node OOM / node offline / sustained >10m above 90%. For p95 spikes that are <5m and not accompanied by OOM/evict — convert to ticket. We can implement with Alertmanager piping to ops-tickets if rule has 'severity: low' and 'duration: <5m'. :white_check_mark:

Ava Chen: Sketch of rule — use promql:
```promql
max_over_time(node_gpu_utilization[5m]) > 0.9
```
But only page if `increase(node_oom_total[15m]) > 0` or `node_up==0` else ticket.

Ravi Patel: We should also group by region and set mute windows for scheduled maintenance windows (us-west patching 0200-0300). Add silences via Alertmanager API.

Maya Ortiz: Also add a "burst smoothing" rule: require the p95 to be above threshold for 3/5 scrapes (e.g., 30s scrape interval *3 = ~90s). Example:
```promql
count_over_time((node_gpu_utilization > 0.9)[2m]) >= 3
```

Luca Marino: Action items: 1) update alert rules with new duration+oom exception (Ravi), 2) add grouping by region + route (Maya), 3) run a 2-week quieting experiment in us-west (Ava). I'll open ticket.

ops-bot: Ticket created: INF-4821 "quieting experiment: us-west transient gpu flares" — assignee: Luca Marino.

Ava Chen: Thanks. We'll monitor SLO impact in the GPU-ops dashboard — link https://grafana.internal/redwood/gpu-ops (useful for reviewers). Ping @oncall for awareness.

Ravi Patel: Can we also add cost tag? Some of these flares are caused by low-priority batch jobs — might be eligible for preemptible nodes. Tagging would help.

Maya Ortiz: Noted. I'll add a label 'workload_class' where possible.

Luca Marino: Thanks all. If no objections, I'll apply change at 09:30 PT and leave a 48h rollback window.

title: #eng-sre: 1747024911
preview:
Rafael Mendes: hey all — prepping ENG-4829 game day. need ideas for KMS outage scenarios + who’s doing what (facilitator/observers/comms).

Sean Gallagher: +1. can we cover both “slow + throttled” and “hard down”? last incident pattern was latency spike → retry amplification.

Allison Grant: scenarios I’d like:
1) p95 latency jump (ex: 200ms → 3s) for decrypt/envelope
2) intermittent throttling (429) at ~5-10% then ramp to 50%
3) full outage (timeouts)
4) AccessDenied due to IAM regression
Also want to validate alerts aren’t paging nonstop in staging :)

Tomáš Novák: add “wrong key id / key disabled” too. we’ve seen misconfig look like InvalidCiphertext / NotFound depending on provider.

Rafael Mendes: yep. let’s list them cleanly:
A) Latency degradation
B) Throttling
C) Full outage/timeouts
D) Permission denied
E) Key disabled / scheduled deletion / wrong ARN
F) Ciphertext/key mismatch during rotation
Anything else?

Kevin Osei: for E/F: we should test the error taxonomy mapping + log fields. I want to confirm we emit `kms.error_category` + provider code consistently + trace span attr.

Ethan Park: for F (rotation mismatch), we can simulate by flipping the “dual-read” flag off prematurely in staging control plane (or use a test key version that can’t decrypt old ciphertext). but we need tight guardrails so we don’t brick staging.

Sean Gallagher: also “KMS returns success but with high tail latency” → should trip circuit breaker based on error/timeout rate? or do we include latency-only breaker?

Allison Grant: breaker needs to be careful. latency-only breaker can cause flapping. maybe we treat timeouts as errors and alert on latency separately.

Rafael Mendes: agreed. for game day we can focus breaker on timeout/error rate. latency spike scenario can validate retry budget + backpressure + dashboards.

Nadia Rahman: from observability side: we should confirm dashboards show:
- request rate to KMS
- error rate by category (Throttling vs AccessDenied vs Timeout)
- breaker state metric
- time spent waiting in retry/backoff
If we don’t have breaker state exported yet we at least need logs.

Marcus Lin: security asks: for D (AccessDenied), confirm the surfaced error is actionable (missing permission / wrong principal) but does not leak sensitive key ARNs in customer-facing paths (Private operators might see more, Hosted less).

Fiona McCarthy: comms-wise: are we running this as a formal incident sim (with #incidents updates) or just internal game day notes?

Rafael Mendes: internal game day but we should practice the muscle memory:
- open a lightweight incident in #incidents (or at least a stub thread)
- post status every ~15 min
- capture timeline + decisions
No external comms.

Connor O'Brien: if we touch any flags/cutovers, I want a checklist + rollback plan written before we start. can we keep it to staging only + one canary env?

Ethan Park: +1. staging only. we can also run the “flag flip” in a sandbox namespace so no shared control plane state is affected.

Tomáš Novák: for C (full outage), what’s expected app behavior? do we fail requests fast or hang until retry budget exhausted?

Sean Gallagher: expectation should be “cap latency”:
- strict per-call timeout (ex 500ms/1s)
- bounded retries w/ jitter
- if breaker trips → immediate fail-fast for new decrypts
But if we have DEK cache, some traffic may still succeed until TTL expires.

Allison Grant: + we should validate we *don’t* melt ourselves (thread pool / goroutines etc.) when KMS is slow. request queue should apply backpressure.

Kevin Osei: can we capture a specific success criterion per scenario? e.g.
- throttling 20% for 10 min → service p99 stays under X, 5xx under Y, and KMS QPS reduces after backpressure
- AccessDenied → alarms fire, logs show remediation hint, oncall can identify missing IAM action within 10 min

Rafael Mendes: love it. rough criteria draft:
A) Latency: dashboards show spike within 2 min; no retry storm; p99 for API doesn’t exceed 2x baseline for >5 min
B) Throttling: error category=Throttling visible; backoff reduces KMS QPS; no cascading 5xx across unrelated routes
C) Outage/timeouts: breaker trips; fail-fast kicks in; oncall sees runbook + mitigation steps; recovery clean (breaker resets)
D) AccessDenied: actionable error surfaced; triage path points to IAM module/runbook; no secret leakage
E) Key disabled/wrong ARN: surfaces as NotFound/Disabled (mapped); operator guidance points to validation/preflight
F) Rotation mismatch: dual-read window catches it; rollback path documented (re-enable dual-read / revert cutover)

Marcus Lin: for F, security requirement is we don’t silently decrypt with “unexpected” keys outside the approved window. but dual-read is explicitly approved during rotation. we should verify audit log event for cutover toggle exists (even in staging).

Nadia Rahman: we can add “audit log event observed in exporter” as a check.

Fiona McCarthy: roles:
- Facilitator: Rafael
- Comms lead: I can do internal incident updates + timekeeping
- Scribe: Tomáš?
- Observers: Allison (alerts/dashboards) + Kevin (logs/taxonomy)
Need an “injector” (the person doing the fault injection / config flips).

Sean Gallagher: I can inject throttling/latency via the test harness (or whatever chaos hook we have in staging). for IAM AccessDenied we might need Jordan/infra but we can simulate by using a principal missing decrypt.

Ethan Park: for flag flips / rotation mismatch simulation, I can be injector for scenario F (with Connor watching release hygiene).

Connor O'Brien: ok I’ll be “change control observer” lol. I’ll keep a rollback checklist and make sure we don’t go off-script.

Rafael Mendes: perfect. proposed schedule: next Tue 10:00–11:30 PT.
Run order: A (latency) → B (throttle) → C (timeout) → D (AccessDenied) → if time, F (rotation mismatch).
E (key disabled/wrong arn) can be a quick follow-up if we still have energy.

Allison Grant: can we ensure alerts are scoped to staging so we don’t page primary oncall? last time staging alerts weren’t muted correctly.

Sean Gallagher: yep. we’ll route to #eng-sre only. IncidentBot can still post but no PagerDuty.

IncidentBot: Reminder: For game days, use channel topic template and link follow-ups to Linear. If you open an incident, tag it as “game-day”.

Kevin Osei: can we link the doc where we’ll take notes + the success criteria list?

Rafael Mendes: dropping a wip doc link: https://drive.google.com/ (kms game day plan)
I’ll paste success criteria + owners there. Also linking ENG-4829 for follow-ups.

Marcus Lin: ask: include a “what would we tell an enterprise customer” section in the after-action. esp. around expected behavior during KMS outages (do we fail closed, do we serve cached data keys, etc.).

Tomáš Novák: +1. will add a blurb + map to runbook actions.

Fiona McCarthy: I’ll prep a comms template:
- start
- scenario injected
- impact observed
- mitigation
- recovery
- action items

Ethan Park: for F: we should pre-create a staging key w/ two versions and a small dataset encrypted under v1. then cutover to v2 without dual-read to trigger mismatch. safer than random production-like data.

Sean Gallagher: cool. for B: I’ll simulate 429s + `Retry-After` behavior. also want to validate our wrapper respects provider hints (where available) and doesn’t hammer.

Allison Grant: and I’ll watch the dashboard panels + alert thresholds. if noisy, we capture tuning notes but we don’t “fix live” unless it’s blocking.

Rafael Mendes: ok, next steps:
- Rafael: finalize agenda + invite
- Sean: confirm fault injection knobs for A/B/C
- Ethan: prep rotation mismatch setup
- Fiona: comms template
- Tomáš: scribe + after-action structure
- Allison/Kevin: define what to screenshot/export for evidence

Connor O'Brien: please add explicit “stop conditions” (if staging starts failing unrelated tests or we see customer-like blast radius).

Rafael Mendes: will do. stop conditions: runaway error rate, inability to recover within 10 min, or impact outside the target service namespace.

Kevin Osei: last thing: can we ensure we test “breaker reset” too? i.e., after recovery, does it heal quickly or do we need manual intervention.

Sean Gallagher: yup. we’ll include recovery phase as part of each scenario.

Rafael Mendes: awesome. I’ll update the doc + ENG-4829 with roles + scenario list today.

Rafael Mendes: hey all — prepping ENG-4829 game day. need ideas for KMS outage scenarios + who’s doing what (facilitator/observers/comms).

Sean Gallagher: +1. can we cover both “slow + throttled” and “hard down”? last incident pattern was latency spike → retry amplification.

Allison Grant: scenarios I’d like:
1) p95 latency jump (ex: 200ms → 3s) for decrypt/envelope
2) intermittent throttling (429) at ~5-10% then ramp to 50%
3) full outage (timeouts)
4) AccessDenied due to IAM regression
Also want to validate alerts aren’t paging nonstop in staging :)

Tomáš Novák: add “wrong key id / key disabled” too. we’ve seen misconfig look like InvalidCiphertext / NotFound depending on provider.

Rafael Mendes: yep. let’s list them cleanly:
A) Latency degradation
B) Throttling
C) Full outage/timeouts
D) Permission denied
E) Key disabled / scheduled deletion / wrong ARN
F) Ciphertext/key mismatch during rotation
Anything else?

Kevin Osei: for E/F: we should test the error taxonomy mapping + log fields. I want to confirm we emit `kms.error_category` + provider code consistently + trace span attr.

Ethan Park: for F (rotation mismatch), we can simulate by flipping the “dual-read” flag off prematurely in staging control plane (or use a test key version that can’t decrypt old ciphertext). but we need tight guardrails so we don’t brick staging.

Sean Gallagher: also “KMS returns success but with high tail latency” → should trip circuit breaker based on error/timeout rate? or do we include latency-only breaker?

Allison Grant: breaker needs to be careful. latency-only breaker can cause flapping. maybe we treat timeouts as errors and alert on latency separately.

- request rate to KMS
- error rate by category (Throttling vs AccessDenied vs Timeout)
- breaker state metric
- time spent waiting in retry/backoff
If we don’t have breaker state exported yet we at least need logs.

title: #eng-sre: 1746902579
preview:
Rafael Mendes: Heads up: staging private cluster is paging *a lot* since we turned on the min-pack rules. Can we do a quick noise review today + decide what’s disabled by default for v1?

Rafael Mendes: Top offenders from last 12h:
- `RedwoodHighLatencyP99` (fires during warm-up + batch size changes)
- `RedwoodGPUUtilizationHigh` (basically constant)
- `KubePodCrashLooping` for log-collector when storage is tight
- `PrometheusTargetMissing` (grafana sidecar / dashboards configmap reload)

Neha Kapoor: +1. I pulled Alertmanager history too. A bunch are “technically true” but not actionable/page-worthy for Private customers.

Sean Gallagher: Can you paste the current thresholds / for: durations? Especially for the latency + GPU ones.

Rafael Mendes: Yep. Current rule snippets (from the staging branch):
```
- alert: RedwoodHighLatencyP99
  expr: histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(redwood_request_latency_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, job)) > 2
  for: 10m
  labels:
    severity: page

- alert: RedwoodGPUUtilizationHigh
  expr: avg_over_time(nvidia_smi_utilization_gpu[5m]) > 0.9
  for: 15m
  labels:
    severity: page
```

Aisha Bello: Latency alert shouldn’t be a raw p99 > X without SLO context. For Private we said we’d do burn-rate style on *availability + latency* SLOs, with `page` only on fast burn.

Aisha Bello: If we keep a simple threshold alert as a backstop, it should be severity=ticket by default, and the `for:` needs to tolerate deploy/warmup.

Tomáš Novák: Also missing runbook links in annotations for some of these. Oncall is going to hate us if alerts page without “first 3 things to check.”

Sergio Costa: Agree. The rules I added were a first pass (copied from hosted-ish defaults). We can tune for Private. Which ones are we OK disabling by default vs keeping but down-severity?

Thomas Baird: For GPU util: `>0.9 for 15m` is *always* true under normal inference load. High utilization is not a failure mode; it’s “good.” We should alert on *sustained saturation with queueing / tail latency impact* or on *OOM / restarts*.

Thomas Baird: Better signals:
- queue depth rising + latency burn
- `DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_USED / FB_TOTAL` (memory pressure) trending to 0.95
- `container_oom_events_total` / `kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total` for runtime pods

Diego Santos: +1. In private installs customers will run hot; “GPU > 90%” is basically a badge of honor.

Neha Kapoor: Proposed policy for v1 minimum pack:
- Page only on: SLO burn (fast), control-plane down, runtime error rate spike, persistent scrape gaps for Redwood targets
- Ticket only on: resource pressure warnings, elevated latency without burn, log pipeline backpressure
- Disabled by default: “nice to know” alerts that have high baseline true rate

Sean Gallagher: For latency: can we switch to something like multiwindow burn? e.g. 2% budget per hour type? Or at least couple with error rate.

Aisha Bello: Yes. For Private guidance we recommended 99.9% API availability + a p95/p99 objective. But alerting should be:
- Fast burn: 5m/1h
- Slow burn: 30m/6h (ticket)

Aisha Bello: If we don’t have enough good recording rules yet, then the fallback should be conservative and non-paging.

Sergio Costa: We *do* have recording rules in PR-18356 branch (redwood_request_success_ratio, redwood_request_rate, redwood_latency_p99). We can build burn on top of those.

Rafael Mendes: The staging cluster is also tiny (2 GPUs) and gets periodic load tests that slam it, so we’re seeing the worst.

Thomas Baird: That’s fine; alerts should still be robust. If our own staging causes constant paging, customers will be worse.

Tomáš Novák: Can we list the alerts and mark them as:
A) on by default
B) on by default but ticket
C) off by default (opt-in)

Neha Kapoor: Here’s my rough cut (happy to edit):
A) ON + PAGE:
- `RedwoodAvailabilityBurnRateFast` (new)
- `RedwoodErrorRateHigh` (page if >X for Y)
- `RedwoodControlPlaneDown` (gateway/auth/policy)
- `RedwoodRuntimePodsNotReady` (but only if >N pods or >10m)
- `RedwoodPrometheusScrapeMissing` for Redwood jobs (for 15m)

B) ON + TICKET:
- `RedwoodLatencyBurnRateSlow` (new)
- `RedwoodHighLatencyP99` (fallback threshold, ticket)
- `RedwoodKVCacheHitRateLow` (if we keep it, needs long window)
- `RedwoodLogForwarderBackpressure` (warn)

C) OFF by default:
- `RedwoodGPUUtilizationHigh`
- `RedwoodCPUThrottlingHigh` (too environment dependent)
- `NodeFilesystemAlmostFull` (customers own node alerts; we can document)

Sean Gallagher: +1, but for `RuntimePodsNotReady` we should exclude deploy rollouts. Use `max_over_time` and require it’s not a rollout in progress (if we have that label/signal).

Diego Santos: We can annotate pods with `app.kubernetes.io/part-of=redwood` and alert only on those. Also, ignore the first 20m post-install / post-upgrade? Installer can set a temporary silence.

Rafael Mendes: Installer silences would be nice but not for this cut. For now, we can bump `for:` durations.

Sergio Costa: For `KubePodCrashLooping` noise: it’s mostly the log collector when PVC fills (staging retention is 24h but PVC is 5Gi). That’s “real,” but it’s not Redwood runtime breaking.

Neha Kapoor: Right. That should be ticket + actionable steps:
1) check PVC usage
2) reduce retention / increase PVC
3) verify exporter sink connectivity (Loki/ELK)

Tomáš Novák: Please embed that into alert annotations. Like:
```
summary: "Log collector crashlooping"
description: "Check PVC usage for <pvc>. If >85%, increase size or lower retention. Verify egress to sink. Runbook: <link>"
```

Thomas Baird: One more: `PrometheusTargetMissing` is firing due to the grafana dashboards sidecar metrics endpoint being blocked by NetworkPolicy. That shouldn’t page either.

Rafael Mendes: In staging it’s our own NP hardening. But customers will also do that. I think scrape-missing for *Redwood runtime/control plane* should be page (since dashboards and alerts become useless), scrape-missing for grafana sidecars should be ticket/off.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Scope the scrape alert to `job=~"redwood-.*"` not `.*`.

Sergio Costa: OK, action items:
- [me] Update rules:
  - remove `RedwoodGPUUtilizationHigh` from default
  - add burn-rate alerts (fast page, slow ticket)
  - downgrade `RedwoodHighLatencyP99` to ticket + raise threshold + longer `for:`
  - tighten scrape-missing selector to Redwood jobs
- [Rafael/Neha] re-run staging with same load test and report pages/tickets

Aisha Bello: For the fallback latency threshold: what’s a sane default? 2s p99 seems too low for private clusters with longer contexts.

Thomas Baird: Also p99 depends heavily on request mix + context length. If we keep it at all, make it p95 and make the threshold something like 5s with a long `for:` and label it “investigate,” not “incident.”

Neha Kapoor: Let’s do:
- p95 > 3s for 30m -> ticket
- p99 > 8s for 30m -> ticket
And rely on burn rate for paging.

Sean Gallagher: Works for me. The burn rate math matters more.

Rafael Mendes: +1. That should stop the constant pages.

Diego Santos: One more ask: ensure alerts don’t include high-cardinality labels (route/model/tenant). Staging is already pushing Prom cardinality.

Thomas Baird: Yes please. Alert labels should be cluster, namespace, job, maybe model_family at most (and even that’s questionable).

Sergio Costa: Ack. I’ll keep annotations generic and point to dashboards where you can drill down.

Tomáš Novák: Also set a consistent severity taxonomy: `page|ticket|info`. Right now I’m seeing `critical` and `warning` mixed in.

Allison Grant: +1 on taxonomy. Private standards doc says `page` + `ticket` + `info`, and runbook links required for page/ticket. Please align before we tag the pack.

Sergio Costa: Got it. I’ll push an update today and link the PR here.

Rafael Mendes: Thanks. Once the PR is up I’ll re-run the staging canary tonight and we’ll validate “no pages unless we break something.” (reaction: :eyes: x2)

Neha Kapoor: Please also test failure injection:
- block egress from runtime to model registry
- kill 1 runtime pod repeatedly
- fill Prometheus PVC to 90%
So we confirm the page/ticket split is sane.

Rafael Mendes: Will do. I’ll post results back in this thread.

Sergio Costa: PR is up: adjusts severities, disables GPU util by default, adds burn-rate alerts using recording rules, and updates scrape selectors. Also added runbook URLs in annotations. Link: https://github.com/redwood-inference/observability-pack/pull/18372 (reaction: :thumbsup: x3)

Rafael Mendes: Nice. I’ll deploy to staging now.

Rafael Mendes: Update after 2h soak + load test: zero pages, 3 tickets:
- slow latency burn (expected during peak load)
- log forwarder backpressure (PVC too small)
- scrape missing for one runtime pod (turned out to be NP misconfig)
This feels *much* better.

Aisha Bello: Great. That’s the behavior we want: pages only for true incidents. Ship it.

Allison Grant: Please make sure the “disabled by default” list is documented in the pack README/values so Support knows what to enable when troubleshooting.

Sergio Costa: Will add to docs in follow-up PR.

Tomáš Novák: And ensure alert annotations include “silence guidance” (when it’s OK to silence) for the ticket-level ones.

Neha Kapoor: +1. Closing the loop here once docs are updated.

```
description: "Check PVC usage for <pvc>. If >85%, increase size or lower retention. Verify egress to sink. Runbook: <link>"
```

Thomas Baird: One more: `PrometheusTargetMissing` is firing due to the grafana dashboards sidecar metrics endpoint being blocked by NetworkPolicy. That shouldn’t page either.

Rafael Mendes: In staging it’s our own NP hardening. But customers will also do that. I think scrape-missing for *Redwood runtime/control plane* should be page (since dashboards and alerts become useless), scrape-missing for grafana sidecars should be ticket/off.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Scope the scrape alert to `job=~"redwood-.*"` not `.*`.

Sergio Costa: OK, action items:
- [me] Update rules:
  - remove `RedwoodGPUUtilizationHigh` from default
  - add burn-rate alerts (fast page, slow ticket)
  - downgrade `RedwoodHighLatencyP99` to ticket + raise threshold + longer `for:`
  - tighten scrape-missing selector to Redwood jobs
- [Rafael/Neha] re-run staging with same load test and report pages/tickets

Aisha Bello: For the fallback latency threshold: what’s a sane default? 2s p99 seems too low for private clusters with longer contexts.

Thomas Baird: Also p99 depends heavily on request mix + context length. If we keep it at all, make it p95 and make the threshold something like 5s with a long `for:` and label it “investigate,” not “incident.”

Neha Kapoor: Let’s do:
- p95 > 3s for 30m -> ticket
- p99 > 8s for 30m -> ticket
And rely on burn rate for paging.

Sean Gallagher: Works for me. The burn rate math matters more.

Rafael Mendes: +1. That should stop the constant pages.

Diego Santos: One more ask: ensure alerts don’t include high-cardinality labels (route/model/tenant). Staging is already pushing Prom cardinality.

Thomas Baird: Yes please. Alert labels should be cluster, namespace, job, maybe model_family at most (and even that’s questionable).

Sergio Costa: Ack. I’ll keep annotations generic and point to dashboards where you can drill down.

Tomáš Novák: Also set a consistent severity taxonomy: `page|ticket|info`. Right now I’m seeing `critical` and `warning` mixed in.

Allison Grant: +1 on taxonomy. Private standards doc says `page` + `ticket` + `info`, and runbook links required for page/ticket. Please align before we tag the pack.

Sergio Costa: Got it. I’ll push an update today and link the PR here.

Rafael Mendes: Thanks. Once the PR is up I’ll re-run the staging canary tonight and we’ll validate “no pages unless we break something.” (reaction: :eyes: x2)

Neha Kapoor: Please also test failure injection:
- block egress from runtime to model registry
- kill 1 runtime pod repeatedly
- fill Prometheus PVC to 90%
So we confirm the page/ticket split is sane.

Rafael Mendes: Will do. I’ll post results back in this thread.

Sergio Costa: PR is up: adjusts severities, disables GPU util by default, adds burn-rate alerts using recording rules, and updates scrape selectors. Also added runbook URLs in annotations. Link: https://github.com/redwood-inference/observability-pack/pull/18372 (reaction: :thumbsup: x3)

Rafael Mendes: Nice. I’ll deploy to staging now.

Rafael Mendes: Update after 2h soak + load test: zero pages, 3 tickets:
- slow latency burn (expected during peak load)
- log forwarder backpressure (PVC too small)
- scrape missing for one runtime pod (turned out to be NP misconfig)
This feels *much* better.

Aisha Bello: Great. That’s the behavior we want: pages only for true incidents. Ship it.

Allison Grant: Please make sure the “disabled by default” list is documented in the pack README/values so Support knows what to enable when troubleshooting.

Sergio Costa: Will add to docs in follow-up PR.

Tomáš Novák: And ensure alert annotations include “silence guidance” (when it’s OK to silence) for the ticket-level ones.

Neha Kapoor: +1. Closing the loop here once docs are updated.

title: #eng-sre: 1746297701
preview:
Neha Kapoor: Kicking off severity mapping for Private minimum pack alerts. We need a crisp “page vs ticket” policy so we don’t ship a pager DOS to customers + to us. Can we align on definitions today?

Aisha Bello: +1. Also want this to line up with SLO burn alerts we’re adding. If we say “Sev2 pages” but it’s just a warning, we’ll regret it.

Allison Grant: Reminder from standards: severity taxonomy is Sev1/Sev2/Sev3 (page, page, ticket) but teams have been inconsistent. We should standardize for Private pack specifically.

Tomáš Novák: From oncall POV: page only for conditions that are actionable immediately and likely customer-impacting. Everything else should create a ticket (and ideally have runbook links in annotations).

Sean Gallagher: + consider that Private clusters are customer-operated. Our oncall can’t fix their NetworkPolicy at 3am. So “page” should be reserved for (a) defects we can mitigate remotely (fallback, config, guidance) or (b) widespread product bug/regression across multiple customers / reference env.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. For minimum pack we’re shipping Prometheus rules into their cluster; by default they might wire alerts to their own paging. We should separate:
- Recommended customer paging (their NOC)
- Redwood Support escalation (ticket)
- Redwood SRE paging (only for Redwood-managed hosted infra / systemic product issue)

Dev Patel: Support angle: if customers page themselves on “Pod restarts > 0” they’ll open a bunch of cases. We need the pack defaults to be conservative + we provide guidance for turning on more.

Neha Kapoor: Ok, propose we define “page-worthy” (Sev1/Sev2) as customer-facing outage / impending outage within minutes, not “something is odd.”

Allison Grant: Can we agree on mapping like:
- Sev1 = hard outage / cannot serve / control plane prevents auth/routing, immediate customer impact
- Sev2 = severe degradation / imminent capacity exhaustion / SLO burn fast
- Sev3 = needs attention but not urgent (ticket)

Aisha Bello: Works, but we need concrete examples per rule type.

Sergio Costa: I have the current draft rules list. The contentious ones are:
- Latency burn (p99)
- Error rate
- GPU saturation
- OOMKills / restart loops
- Prometheus scrape down / exporter down
- Disk pressure for Prometheus PVC
- Log forwarder dropping

Ethan Park: Control plane side: if `policy-engine` or `auth-proxy` down, that’s basically “nothing works” (Sev1). If `model-registry-sync` failing, that’s usually degraded ops (Sev3) unless it blocks traffic.

Logan Wright: Metrics pipeline: scrape failures are tricky. If Prometheus can’t scrape Redwood components, dashboards/alerts go blind. That’s bad, but is it page? Maybe Sev2 if it’s widespread AND not just one target.

Tomáš Novák: In practice: “observability broken” should not page at night unless it hides an active outage. Otherwise people wake up and can’t fix customer network anyway.

Neha Kapoor: Let’s do a rule-by-rule mapping. Starting with API availability / error rate.

Aisha Bello: Suggest:
- Availability/error rate (5xx) high for 5m: Sev1 if >10% across all routes (or overall) and request volume above min threshold. Sev2 if 2–10%.
- Latency: Sev2 on fast burn (e.g., 14x burn rate for 10m) and Sev3 on slow burn.

Sean Gallagher: +1 on using burn rate windows. Also need a volume guard so a single customer hitting a bad prompt doesn’t trip global alerts.

Sergio Costa: For Private we intentionally removed high-cardinality labels (route/model/tenant) from the default aggregate alerts. We can base on `service="api-gateway"` + `component` only.

Rafael Mendes: GPU saturation:
- Sev2 if GPU util > 95% AND queue depth rising for 10m (actionable: scale / add replicas / reduce max_tokens)
- Sev3 if GPU util high but queue stable
- OOMKills/restarts: Sev2 if crashlooping a serving runtime (>=3 restarts / 10m) AND no healthy replicas. Sev3 if restarts but service still has healthy capacity.

Dev Patel: That matches support experience. Customers tolerate “GPU hot” warnings; they panic when inference stops.

Ethan Park: Control plane alerts:
- `api-gateway` down (no endpoints) Sev1
- `policy-engine` down Sev1
- `installer-controller` failing reconcile Sev3 (unless blocking upgrade/rollback in progress -> Sev2)

Allison Grant: Prometheus/Grafana health:
- Prometheus target down: Sev3 default. Provide a separate “observability blind” composite alert as Sev2 but DISABLED by default.
- Prometheus disk filling: Sev2 when <10% free / <24h until full (since it will break monitoring), Sev3 at <20%.

Tomáš Novák: Important: Anything “disabled by default” needs to be explicit in docs and values.yaml. Otherwise customers assume we forgot to ship it.

Logan Wright: Log forwarder dropping/backpressure:
- Sev3 by default. If drop rate > X% for >30m, then Sev2 (still ticket, not page?)

Sean Gallagher: I’d keep log drop Sev3 unless customer uses it for audit/compliance. But that’s out of min pack scope; we can note “if logs required for compliance, tighten thresholds.”

Neha Kapoor: So for the pack we’ll recommend:
Customer paging (default): Sev1+Sev2 only (outage/degradation). Sev3 routes to ticket/email.
Redwood internal paging: none from customer clusters (minimum pack), unless escalated by Support for systemic product bug.

Dev Patel: + please add alert annotations: “Impact / likely causes / first steps / when to open Support case” and link to runbook.

Sergio Costa: I’ll update rule annotations with `runbook_url` and `summary`/`description` fields. Also add a `customer_action` hint where relevant.

Aisha Bello: One more: SLO burn alerts should be Sev2 (page) only for FAST burn. Slow burn = Sev3.

Rafael Mendes: Works. Also for noisy ones (CPU/mem on pods), can we keep them Sev3 and disabled by default? Those were the biggest source of noise in staging.

Allison Grant: Yes. Resource pressure alerts that aren’t directly correlated with serving degradation should be Sev3 and opt-in. We can keep OOM/restarts enabled since that correlates strongly.

Tomáš Novák: Can we put a short “philosophy” line in the doc: “We alert on symptoms (errors/latency/saturation), not on raw utilization.”

Neha Kapoor: Capturing decisions:
1) Sev1 = hard outage (control plane/API down, no serving capacity) -> page (customer + their oncall)
2) Sev2 = severe degradation / imminent outage (fast SLO burn, queue growth with saturation, disk near-full) -> page
3) Sev3 = informational/action-needed (scrapes down, moderate resource pressure, log pipeline issues, slow burn) -> ticket/email
4) Observability-blind composite alert: shipped but disabled by default
5) Add volume guards + burn-rate style for latency/error alerts

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: please also make sure doc explicitly says Redwood doesn’t receive these alerts by default in Private (customers own alert routing). We’ve had confusion on that.

Dev Patel: +1. I’ll add a line for Support: “If Sev1 persists >15m after customer action, open Sev1 Support case” (or similar).

Neha Kapoor: Great. Sergio/Allison can you sync on final wording + thresholds and I’ll drop this into the minimum pack doc + alert-noise spreadsheet.

Sergio Costa: On it. Will post PR link once updated.

Allison Grant: I’ll update the standards snippet for Private pack and add a small table mapping each shipped alert to default severity + enabled/disabled.

Rafael Mendes: Once that lands, I’ll rerun in staging and sanity-check pages vs tickets. :pager:

title: #eng-sre: 1741586112
preview:
Sean Gallagher: Heads up: we’re going to start doing parallel sales demos off the same “demo tenant” next week and I’m worried we’re under-sizing capacity + quotas. Last 2 dry runs were fine with 1 demo, but 2 in flight could easily trigger 429s / queue spikes.

Markus Klein: yep. had a call yesterday where we ran the live scenario + synthetic traffic at the same time and it got close. biggest fear is a mid-demo stall.

Allison Grant: What are the current assumptions? How many concurrent demos do we want to support as “normal” vs burst?

Sean Gallagher: Proposal from SRE side:
- Treat demo env like prod-lite: dedicated GPU pool (noisy neighbor protection)
- Hard caps via rate limits (token/sec + concurrent reqs) per demo project
- Synthetic traffic generator must run in a separate project/route w/ its own quota so it can’t starve the live demo

Devon Price: +1 on separating synthetic traffic. Right now bootstrap script puts everything in the same project unless you override.

Ethan Park: quotas/rate limits: we can do per-project + per-api-key limits. we should avoid “org-level everything” for demo because parallel demos become coupled.

Mei Lin: For dashboard seeding: traffic generator can be shaped (low QPS, steady) but if someone kicks it to “heavy” preset it’ll absolutely eat the pool. We can lock presets in demo env.

Ravi Menon: On routing side, we can also pin demo routes to a specific capacity tier + region (and allow controlled fallback). But if fallback is enabled and primary is saturated, it could spill to shared capacity unless we’re careful.

Sean Gallagher: exactly. requirement for parallel demos: demo capacity should be isolated from shared hosted where possible. if we do allow fallback, it should be within demo pool regions first, then last-resort shared (with a big warning).

Allison Grant: What size pool are we talking? We need something that’s defensible + doesn’t explode cost.

Sean Gallagher: rough math (hand-wavy but directionally):
- “standard 30 min demo” has 2–3 spiky moments (streaming chat + tool call + structured output) + console views
- if we assume 2 concurrent demos + background synthetic traffic, we should size for the spiky moments, not the average
- I’d like us to commit to N=3 concurrent demos without paging people

Markus Klein: N=3 would save us. we already had two AEs asking for same time slots next week.

Devon Price: If we can’t get a truly dedicated GPU pool fast, can we at least cap concurrency so the API fails “nicely” (fast 429 + clear message) instead of hanging/slow streaming?

Sean Gallagher: yes. rate-limit caps are the safety valve:
- cap concurrent streaming requests per key
- cap tokens/sec per key
- cap max prompt tokens to avoid someone pasting a whole doc mid-demo
We should also tune the demo app to retry w/ jitter and show the “retries + backoff” story instead of silently spinning.

Ethan Park: can implement limit config in demo org defaults. also can make reset/healthcheck endpoint print current quota settings so SE can validate before going live.

Allison Grant: please do. We should add alerting too: “demo pool utilization > X% for Y minutes” and “429 rate > Z” to a GTM-friendly channel.

Mei Lin: synthetic traffic job: I can add a guard that prevents running “heavy” preset in demo env unless you pass `--force` (and maybe only allow from CI).

Ravi Menon: + add routing policy so the synthetic traffic uses a cheaper model variant. It doesn’t need to be the flagship route, just needs to populate dashboards.

Markus Klein: +1. for live demo we want the flagship, but dashboards can be fed by anything as long as distributions look realistic.

Sean Gallagher: Action items I’ll open / tie off:
1) Dedicated demo GPU pool sizing proposal + cost estimate (SRE) (I’ll draft)
2) Rate-limit caps per demo project/key (Ethan + Devon)
3) Separate synthetic traffic into its own project + quota (Mei + Devon)
4) Alerting: utilization + 429 + dashboard freshness (Allison)
5) Routing guardrails so fallback doesn’t escape isolation unexpectedly (Ravi)

Allison Grant: can you link the Linear? want to make sure we don’t duplicate.

Sean Gallagher: linking here: ENG-4894 (parallel demos support) + ENG-4879 (demo alerts). Will comment w/ these notes. :thumbsup:

Devon Price: also worth adding this to the reset runbook: “before demo: run healthcheck and confirm demo pool green + quotas sane”. we’ve been doing it ad hoc.

Markus Klein: yes pls. SEs need a simple “green/red” output, not a wall of numbers.

Ethan Park: I can add a short summary section to healthcheck output:
- capacity tier: OK/DEGRADED
- quotas: OK (X req/min, Y tok/s)
- dashboard ingestion: OK (last event < 5m)

Allison Grant: :+1: If we can make the failure mode “we hit our demo safety limits” (not “platform is flaky”), that’s already a huge win.

Sean Gallagher: agreed. goal is predictable degradation. I’ll post an update once we have a pool proposal + initial caps in place.

title: #eng-sre: 1741495210
preview:
Sean Gallagher: proposing we lock demo-env SLOs + error budget policy this week so we have something concrete for alerting + escalation. this is for the *demo tenant* only (not prod). looking for owner confirms + where pages go.

Sean Gallagher: draft SLOs (feel free to bikeshed numbers, but we need something):
1) Demo API availability: 99.7% weekly (request success excluding customer-induced 4xx)
2) Demo API latency: p95 < 1.8s TTFT for streaming, p95 < 6.5s for 256 output tokens (measured on seeded synthetic workload)
3) Model pin integrity: 99.9% of requests routed to pinned version (fallback allowed only to approved variant list)
4) Console dashboard freshness: seeded project has non-empty graphs for last 15m window 99% of the time during business hours (Mon-Fri)
5) Reset + healthcheck: `demo reset` completes < 8 min p95; healthcheck green >= 99% of runs

Sean Gallagher: error budget policy (draft):
- Burn > 25% in 24h => create incident + block new demo bookings until mitigated (unless we have a validated backup path)
- Burn > 50% in 24h => page demo-oncall (SRE primary) + SE owner, switch to backup mode (screenshots/recording)
- Any Sev2 demo event (live demo impacted) => postmortem-lite within 48h

Sean Gallagher: open questions:
- who owns each SLO (eng owner + GTM owner)
- where do alerts route (I’d like a dedicated alias + channel, not #sales)
- what qualifies as “demo Sev2” vs “annoying but ok”

Allison Grant: +1 to having explicit numbers. for alert routing: can we do `#demo-oncall` + PagerDuty service “Demo Environment” (SRE on-call) and a non-paging heads-up to `#sales`?

Markus Klein: pls yes on not paging #sales directly 🙏. GTM owner can be me for “live demo impact” definition + comms. but we need clear “what to do right now” playbook.

Talia Benson: for owners I suggest:
- API availability/latency: Sean (SRE) + Ethan (control plane)
- Console freshness: Dylan/Chloe + Allison for alerting
- Reset p95: Devon (automation) but I can help chase
- Model pin integrity: Mina/Ravi (routing) but looping them in separately
also can we scope “business hours” by region? EMEA demos are happening too.

Ethan Park: availability/latency numbers look reasonable. caveat: we should exclude planned maintenance windows + make sure healthcheck uses the same auth path as SEs (RBAC has bitten us).

Dylan Brooks: dashboard freshness SLO: I’m okay owning the metric definition, but the dependency is ingestion + the synthetic traffic generator. if it’s “graphs not empty,” we need a precise query (time range + project selector) to avoid false positives.

Chloe Martin: +1. empty-state bug from last week was UI default time range. I’d rather the SLO be “ingestion has events in last 15m AND dashboard query returns >0 points” so we can tell if it’s pipeline vs UI.

Sean Gallagher: good call. we can phrase it as two signals:
A) telemetry ingestion: events present in demo project within last 15m
B) console query smoke test: dashboard API returns non-empty series for latency + cost panels
page on A failing; ticket on B failing (unless it’s during a live demo).

Allison Grant: I can wire A as a paging alert (PD) with a 10m “for” to avoid flapping. B as Slack to `#demo-oncall` + auto-create Jira if it persists > 2h.

Markus Klein: re “demo Sev2” definition:
- any failure that forces us off the live path (429s, auth failure, dashboards empty when we promised to show them, streaming disconnects)
- or adds >5 min of awkward troubleshooting on the call
if we switch to backup screenshots smoothly in <2 min, maybe that’s Sev3?

Talia Benson: +1. also add “reset exceeds 15 min and blocks back-to-back demos” as Sev2 (it’s a GTM schedule killer).

Sean Gallagher: cool. proposal:
Sev2 (Demo): live customer/prospect call impacted OR prevents scheduled demo from starting within 5 min. Sev3: internal dry run impacted or workaround available <2 min.

Ethan Park: escalation flow suggestion:
- SE running demo posts in `#demo-oncall` with “DEMO-HELP” prefix
- SRE on-call acknowledged in 2 min
- if auth/RBAC => Ethan (control plane) as secondary
- if dashboards => Dylan/Chloe secondary
- if routing/model => Mina/Ravi secondary

Allison Grant: can we get a single doc with “if X then Y” + links? otherwise nobody will remember.

Sean Gallagher: yep. I’ll draft a confluence page today with:
- SLO table + owners
- alert routing
- Sev2/Sev3 definitions
- escalation contacts
and I’ll post for sign-off. need explicit “✅” from owners by EOD Thu so we can ship alert rules.

Markus Klein: ✅ from me on GTM ownership + sev definitions. once we have the doc I’ll socialize with SE team.

Dylan Brooks: ✅ on owning the dashboard SLO definition + smoke test endpoint requirements.

Chloe Martin: ✅ (console backend) as secondary on dashboard issues.

Ethan Park: ✅ (control plane/auth) as secondary on auth + quota weirdness.

Talia Benson: ✅ I’ll track the action items + make sure Mina/Ravi/Devon are looped on owners for model pin + reset.

Sean Gallagher: awesome. I’ll follow up with the draft + proposed PD service name + schedule. thanks all.

title: #eng-sre: 1741124411
preview:
Sean Gallagher: For LTS pilot + GA we need *separate* dashboards/alerts for LTS traffic and patch rollouts. Right now everything rolls up under runtime overall, which makes it hard to tell if 1.18-lts is healthy vs mainline. Can we scope what we want in SLO toolkit / dd dashboards this week?

Sergio Costa: Yep. I’ve been thinking we need 3 layers:
1) LTS fleet health (by channel=lts)
2) Patch rollout monitoring (by runtime_version=1.18.1-lts, region, cluster)
3) Customer-impact view (Dedicated/Private tenants on LTS)

Allison Grant: +1. Biggest gap: latency SLO views split by version. We’ve had cases where global p95 was fine but the new patch had regressions on A100 only.

Connor O'Brien: Release Eng POV: we need something we can use during patch week. Ideally a “release cockpit” dashboard that shows error budget burn + p50/p95 + throughput per GPU SKU *for the exact tag* we’re rolling.

Rafael Mendes: Also alerts need to page the right people. During pilot, on-call gets paged for generic runtime SLO breaches and we lose time figuring out if it’s the LTS rollout or just noisy neighbors.

Sean Gallagher: Agreed. Let’s define what labels exist / need to exist. Do we already have runtime_version label in metrics everywhere?

Allison Grant: Not everywhere. In inference-gateway we have `model`, `route`, `customer_tier`, `region`. Runtime emits `build_sha` but not consistently surfaced through OTel pipeline. We *do* have `image_tag` on k8s metrics but it’s painful to join.

Sergio Costa: For SLO toolkit, easiest is to standardize a dimension like:
- `runtime_channel`: main | lts
- `runtime_version`: 1.18.1-lts (string tag)
- `runtime_branch`: runtime-1.18-lts (optional)
We can do it first in the request metrics (gateway) + runtime process metrics (exporter).

Marcus Reed: Perf suite side: perf-canary outputs a summary artifact with “expected p95” and baseline delta for the tag. Could we link that from the dashboard?

Connor O'Brien: Yes please. Even just a URL annotation on the Grafana/Datadog board like “Perf gate report: <link>”. During RC signoff it’s a bunch of tabs.

Jonas Weber: For alerting, we should avoid paging on raw latency spikes during rollout because canaries can be noisy. Suggest:
- Page on *error budget burn rate* for LTS SLO (multi-window burn)
- Warn on p95 latency regression > threshold compared to pre-rollout baseline (needs baseline capture)
- Separate “rollout anomaly” notification to #eng-releases (non-page)

Sean Gallagher: What SLO do we want for LTS specifically? Same as Dedicated? Or separate objective?

Rafael Mendes: For now, same objective but separate SLO entity so burn is attributable. Otherwise LTS incidents get lost in the aggregate.

Allison Grant: We can define:
- LTS Inference Availability SLO (5xx + timeouts) for traffic tagged runtime_channel=lts
- LTS Latency SLO (p95 under target) same filter
Then build the “error budget” panel specifically for LTS.

Sergio Costa: Implementation detail: SLO toolkit can create per-slice SLOs if we have reliable tags. I can add a new SLO set `runtime-lts-*` and wire to dd monitors.

Connor O'Brien: Release flow question: for a given patch we do staged rollout (internal -> pilot tenants -> broader). Can we get a view by stage?

Sean Gallagher: Stage is basically “deployment ring”. Do we have ring labels in clusters?

Allison Grant: We do for Hosted (`ring=dev|staging|prod-canary|prod`) but Dedicated/Private is different. Dedicated has `cluster_type=dedicated` + `tenant_id` (hashed), and Private doesn’t always report tenant labels (by design).

Rafael Mendes: For Private, we likely can’t rely on tenant_id. But for our managed Private (VPC) we can at least track by environment ID. For true on-prem, dashboards are customer-side anyway.

Sergio Costa: I propose dashboards:
A) “Runtime LTS - Global” (Hosted+Dedicated) filtered runtime_channel=lts
B) “Runtime LTS - Rollout” with breakdown by region + ring + runtime_version
C) “Runtime LTS - Dedicated top tenants” (top N by traffic, error rate, p95) gated to internal-only

Connor O'Brien: Works. Also need a “mandatory patch” indicator: if we declare a patch mandatory due to Sev1/security, we’ll want to monitor adoption. Can we graph % of LTS traffic by runtime_version?

Allison Grant: Yes if runtime_version exists. That chart is super useful. It’s basically a stacked area: requests by runtime_version. During rollout you want to see the curve shift.

Marcus Reed: And overlay perf metrics by GPU: tokens/sec, queue time, batch size distribution. Scheduler regressions often show up as queue_time spikes not just p95.

Sean Gallagher: Queue time = the scheduler wait time metric? Do we have that in dd?

Allison Grant: We have `scheduler_queue_ms` histogram from runtime, but it’s currently not tagged with version (only host). That’s one of the action items.

Sergio Costa: Ok action items:
1) Add/propagate `runtime_channel` + `runtime_version` tags to gateway request metrics.
2) Propagate same tags to runtime exporter metrics (scheduler queue, batch metrics, kv cache, etc).
3) Create SLO toolkit config for LTS availability + latency.
4) Dashboards + monitors for:
- LTS burn rate (paging)
- LTS 5xx spike (paging)
- LTS p95 regression vs baseline (warn -> page only if burn confirms)
- Rollout adoption (% traffic by version) (non-page + #eng-releases)

Rafael Mendes: On paging: who is primary? SRE on-call + runtime on-call? Or SRE only?

Sean Gallagher: For pilot: SRE primary, runtime secondary. Once stable we can revisit. But the page message needs to say “LTS SLO burn” explicitly.

Connor O'Brien: Please also post to #eng-releases automatically when LTS monitor triggers during patch week. We’ll have Release Eng watching but not necessarily on pager rotation.

Sergio Costa: We can route:
- PagerDuty: `runtime-sre` service
- Slack: #eng-releases + #eng-runtime (warning)
- Add runbook links in monitor body (release cockpit + rollback steps)

Allison Grant: Runbook link should point to the new LTS patch runbook section “Rollout monitoring + rollback signals”. Connor can you drop link once it’s in Confluence?

Connor O'Brien: Will do. It’s in draft now, will be published before first patch.

Datadog Bot: Allison Grant added a comment on monitor draft "[WIP] Runtime LTS Latency Burn Rate".

Jonas Weber: One more: thresholds. For p95 regression, I’d suggest per-workload thresholds are hard; start with absolute for core routes:
- chat.completions p95 > baseline * 1.10 for 15m (warn)
- embeddings p95 > baseline * 1.15 for 15m (warn)
But don’t page unless error budget burn rate > 2x as well.

Allison Grant: Agree. Also need GPU-specific panels. A100 vs H100 can diverge. Can we tag GPU SKU from node labels into metrics?

Sergio Costa: We already have `gpu_sku` in infra metrics; request metrics don’t have it. Might be joinable by cluster, but not ideal. I’ll see if we can enrich at collector level.

Sean Gallagher: Timeline: can we have v0 dashboard + 2 monitors by end of week for pilot readiness?

Sergio Costa: Yes for gateway + SLO. Runtime exporter tagging might slip depending on how invasive. I can do v0 using k8s `image_tag` + host mapping, then iterate.

Connor O'Brien: If v0 is “good enough for patch week”, that’s fine. But we should track the proper tagging as a must-have for GA.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also please add a small panel for “release annotations”: when we start rollout / pause / rollback. Otherwise people guess based on graphs.

Allison Grant: We can add manual annotations from the release script or just a button in dd notebook. Connor/Devon might be able to automate.

Connor O'Brien: I’ll ask Devon Price if we can have release automation drop an annotation with runtime_version + stage.

Sergio Costa: Cool. I’ll open a PR in slo-toolkit for `runtime-lts-*` SLOs + start a dashboard draft and share link here.

Sean Gallagher: Thanks. Please include in the dashboard:
- error budget remaining (7d/30d)
- burn rate (1h/6h)
- p50/p95 latency by route
- 5xx + timeouts
- scheduler queue time
- adoption by runtime_version
- breakdown by region + ring

Allison Grant: I’ll work with Sergio on the panels + make sure the metric queries match what perf gates look at.

Sergio Costa: Posted draft board link in #eng-sre pins once it’s not embarrassing :)

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: Let’s make sure the monitors have clear “what to do” text. Half the battle is not digging through 5 dashboards at 2am.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Add “if during rollout: pause at ring X” and “rollback to previous LTS patch” guidance in the alert body. We’ll iterate after first patch retro.

title: #eng-sre: 1741023321
preview:
Sean Gallagher: We need to lock perf regression thresholds for Runtime LTS gates this week. Current proposal in doc is a bit hand-wavy. Can we align on p50/p95 + throughput + what to do when benches are noisy?

Allison Grant: +1. For LTS we should bias towards catching p95 regressions even if it means occasional reruns.

Connor O'Brien: Release Eng perspective: thresholds need to be deterministic enough that patch week isn’t "rerun until green". Also need an explicit waiver path (who can override, what evidence required).

Marcus Reed: Here’s what we’ve been using informally for mainline perf-canary:
- latency p50 regression <= +5%
- latency p95 regression <= +8%
- tok/s throughput regression <= -3%
…but the variance on A100 runners is sometimes +/-4% on p95, depending on contention.

Jonas Weber: For LTS, I’d tighten p95, but only if we also change methodology (more repetitions / better aggregation). Otherwise we’ll just create flakes.

Claire Dubois: Agree. Our measurement noise is dominated by runner contention + occasional thermal / clock drift. If we keep single-run comparisons, any threshold <~6% on p95 will be flaky.

Rafael Mendes: From incident history: p95 regressions are what trigger customer escalations. I’d rather have a gating system that forces a second look (rerun + manual review) than ship a p95 regression because "it was within +8%".

Sean Gallagher: Ok, let’s separate:
(1) what numbers constitute "hard fail" vs "needs review"
(2) rerun policy
(3) waiver policy.

Kira Volkov: I can implement whatever logic in the gate parser, but need exact rules. Right now perf-canary outputs a JSON summary w/ deltas vs baseline.

Marcus Reed: Suggestion:
- Run 3 iterations per benchmark scenario, use median for p50/p95, and mean for throughput.
- Compare against baseline median-of-3.
That will cut noise without exploding runtime too much.

Claire Dubois: median-of-3 is good. For throughput, use median too. Mean is sensitive to one slow run.

Jonas Weber: +1 median everywhere. Also we should pin GPU clocks (where possible) + isolate runners, but that’s infra work.

Allison Grant: Thresholds proposal (LTS):
- p50 latency: warn at +3%, fail at +6%
- p95 latency: warn at +4%, fail at +7%
- throughput tok/s: warn at -2%, fail at -5%
This assumes median-of-3.

Connor O'Brien: That seems workable. Q: do we apply per-workload, or aggregate across canonical workloads?

Marcus Reed: Per-workload + per-GPU SKU. We’ve seen regressions only show up on H100 for long-context.

Sergio Costa: If we do per-workload + per-SKU, we need to be strict about the benchmark set. Otherwise every patch will be 2h of benchmarks.

Sean Gallagher: Right, scope it. For LTS patches, canonical set should be:
- short prompts (128-512)
- mixed prompts (128-4k)
- long context (8k+)
- with/without streaming
and GPU SKUs: A100 80GB + H100 SXM (at least those two).

Claire Dubois: Please also include one "scheduler stress" scenario (bursty arrivals). That’s where p95 gets weird.

Marcus Reed: We already have a bursty queue scenario in perf-canary, it’s just not part of default gating. I can add it.

Perf Canary Bot: Benchmark summary available: https://perf-canary.redwood.dev/runs/1741025512 (note: runner contention detected on a100-03)

Rafael Mendes: lol bot calling out contention. That’s exactly why we need a standard "noisy run" procedure.

Allison Grant: Noisy run rule of thumb:
- If contention detected OR variance across the 3 iterations > 5% on p95, auto-mark as "inconclusive" and require rerun on a different runner.

Kira Volkov: I can code:
- compute coefficient of variation (CV) across the 3 runs; if CV > threshold, flag as noisy.
But we need to pick numbers.

Jonas Weber: CV on p95 is usually high even when fine. Maybe simpler:
- if max(p95) - min(p95) > 7% of median(p95) => noisy.
Same for throughput.

Claire Dubois: +1. CV is messy with small N.

Sean Gallagher: Let’s lock:
- Noisy if (max-min)/median > 0.07 for p95 OR throughput.
- Noisy if perf-canary marks "contention detected".
Noisy => rerun once. If rerun also noisy, manual review required (don’t block forever).

Connor O'Brien: How does manual review work? We need an explicit waiver process that’s auditable.

Rafael Mendes: Proposed waiver:
- Release manager (Connor or delegate) can request waiver.
- Requires: perf owner (Marcus) + SRE on-call manager (Rafael or Sean) sign-off.
- Requires attaching evidence: links to both runs + justification (e.g. change is scheduler-only, regression isolated to non-customer workload, etc.).
- Must add a line item in LTS release notes under "Performance" that a waiver was granted + what to monitor.

Allison Grant: +1, and waiver should be time-bounded: "waiver applies only to this patch" not permanent.

Marcus Reed: Also add: if fail threshold exceeded (not just warn), waiver requires a third run on isolated runner (if available). I don’t want us waving away a -8% throughput hit.

Sergio Costa: And for monitoring, we should tag the rollout w/ an "LTS-perf-waiver" annotation so dashboards can be watched. I can add that to SLO dashboards.

Kira Volkov: So logic:
- WARN band: gate is yellow -> requires perf review comment but can proceed.
- FAIL band: gate is red -> blocks unless waiver.
- If NOISY: gate is gray -> rerun required.
That ok?

Sean Gallagher: Yes. But define what "requires perf review comment" means.

Connor O'Brien: For WARN, we’ll require a comment in the release PR:
"Perf gate WARN on <workload>/<sku> with delta <x>. Reviewed by <name>. Acceptable because <reason>. Monitoring plan: <link>."
No separate waiver needed.

Rafael Mendes: For FAIL + waiver, do it as a checklist item with sign-off lines. Something like:
- [ ] Waiver requested by:
- [ ] Approved by Perf (Marcus):
- [ ] Approved by SRE (Sean/Rafael):
- [ ] Additional run link (if required):
- [ ] Release notes updated:

Allison Grant: Can we also say: any p95 fail (> +7%) on canonical long-context workload is basically "no waiver unless exec sponsor"? That’s the one that hurts.

Sean Gallagher: I’d rather avoid exec escalation in the written policy. Maybe: "waivers for p95 fail on long-context require explicit SRE Director approval"… but we don’t have that role listed for this project.

Connor O'Brien: Let’s keep it to named roles for now. We can state: "p95 fail on long-context requires SRE + Runtime leads sync before proceeding". (Not a formal new approver, just required discussion.)

Marcus Reed: Works.

Claire Dubois: One more: baseline drift. If baseline is older than 30 days, the "delta" might be less meaningful due to driver updates / infra changes. Can we pin baseline to the LTS branch cut tag?

Connor O'Brien: Yes, baseline should be "branch cut baseline" for that LTS line, updated only when we intentionally re-baseline (with note). That’s already in the draft, but we’ll make it explicit.

Kira Volkov: Cool. I’ll update the gate config with:
- warn/fail thresholds
- noisy detection and rerun required
- output includes links to runs + which runner

Sergio Costa: I’ll add a dashboard panel that overlays release tag + perf gate status + p95 deltas during rollout.

Sean Gallagher: Summarizing what we’re locking (reply if objections):
1) Method: median-of-3 runs per scenario/SKU for p50/p95/throughput.
2) Threshold bands (LTS):
   - p50 latency: warn +3%, fail +6%
   - p95 latency: warn +4%, fail +7%
   - throughput tok/s: warn -2%, fail -5%
3) Noisy criteria: contention detected OR (max-min)/median > 7% on p95 or throughput.
   - Noisy => rerun once on different runner if possible.
4) WARN handling: perf owner review comment in release PR.
5) FAIL handling: blocks unless waiver.
6) Waiver: requested by release manager; approvals required from Perf owner + SRE (Sean/Rafael). Evidence: run links, rationale, monitoring plan, and release notes entry.
7) Extra scrutiny: any p95 FAIL on long-context workload requires synchronous review between Release Eng + Perf owner + SRE before waiver.

Allison Grant: +1 :thumbsup:

Rafael Mendes: +1

Marcus Reed: +1 (and I’ll make sure long-context scenario is included in the canonical set for LTS)

Connor O'Brien: +1, I’ll incorporate into the LTS patch runbook + release PR template.

Claire Dubois: +1, thanks. Please also capture the baseline policy in the doc (baseline pinned to branch cut; rebaseline is an explicit change w/ sign-off).

Sean Gallagher: Will do. Kira, once config lands, tag me on the PR.

Kira Volkov: yep, will do today.

- tok/s throughput regression <= -3%

title: #eng-sre: 1741015402
preview:
Allison Grant: Dropping the cache telemetry incident runbook here for final review before we call it “oncall-ready”: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/PLAT/cache-telemetry-pipeline-incident-runbook

Allison Grant: Would love eyes on (1) symptoms/triage flow, (2) ownership + escalation, (3) what we consider a “real cache regression” vs telemetry missing.

Sean Gallagher: Skimmed. This is solid. Biggest ask: make the first 5 mins super explicit. Like “is data missing?” check before we chase cache runtime issues.

Aisha Bello: +1. Also can we add a quick note about expected diurnal dips / workload mix changes so we don’t page ourselves into oblivion.

Sergio Costa: I’ll add a top section: “Sanity checks (no paging)” w/ links to the ingestion drop panel + metric volume panels.

Logan Wright: On telemetry side, the failure modes list is accurate. One tweak: call out label-drop/cardinality guard as its own class of incident.

Logan Wright: We’ve already had a “looks like 0 hits” that was actually ingestion rejecting high-card label combos.

Nadia Rahman: + add explicit mention of allowed label set + where to find it. People always ask “why is route missing?”

Allison Grant: Yep. I’ll add: “If you see sudden zeros but request volume is normal, check cardinality guard drops”.

Omar El-Sayed: Runtime comments:
- In the “hit-rate drop” section, recommend checking miss_reason breakdown before changing cache knobs.
- We should be careful with the mitigation guidance “increase max_prefix_cache_bytes” — that can blow GPU mem on small cards.

Omar El-Sayed: Maybe phrase as: "consider increasing max_prefix_cache_bytes within approved profile; validate memory pressure gauge".

Sean Gallagher: Agree. Don’t want oncall blindly flipping runtime flags. Keep mitigations conservative.

Ethan Park: Join keys: runbook currently says “model_version from gateway + runtime are consistent”. That’s true post-18426, but during rollout we’ll have mixed tags.

Ethan Park: Suggest adding note: “If model_version is missing, slice by model_family/model_id as fallback; don’t conclude version mismatch until rollout complete”.

Connor O'Brien: +1, and add rollout context: while canarying, some regions may lag. Oncall should check release status first.

Redwood Deploy Bot: Release status: runtime-cache-telemetry v0.9.3-canary is ACTIVE in us-east-1, eu-west-1. Next wave scheduled in 6h.

Allison Grant: Great call. I’ll add a “Check release state” step + link to the deploy dashboard.

Leila Farouk: QA note: runbook should mention counter resets on pod restarts. We saw false “hit-rate cliff” when denominator restarted.

Leila Farouk: If you can add “look for reset markers / use rate() not raw counter” in queries, that’d help.

Sergio Costa: I can update the example queries in the runbook to use `rate()` / `increase()` and include a snippet.

Aisha Bello: Also the alert section: can we clarify when it’s a page vs ticket?

Sean Gallagher: Paging policy:
- Page only if (a) cache_efficiency_guardrail breached AND (b) request volume stable AND (c) telemetry health green.
- If telemetry health red -> ticket Telemetry, don’t page runtime.

Sean Gallagher: Put that logic in the runbook and also in the alert annotations.

Allison Grant: Noted. I’ll add a simple decision table.

Logan Wright: Ownership: who owns ongoing runbook maintenance? Telemetry docs change a lot.

Allison Grant: Proposal:
- Primary owner: Observability/SRE (me) for the runbook structure + triage flow.
- Section owners: Telemetry ingestion (Logan) + Runtime metrics semantics (Omar) + Tagging/join keys (Ethan).
- Sean as approver for paging policy changes.

Omar El-Sayed: Works. For runtime semantics, loop in Samir when we change miss_reason taxonomy.

Nadia Rahman: I can be backup for schema/label standards. Please add link to the naming/cardinality policy doc.

Ethan Park: I’ll take tagging/join keys section. I’ll add a short “how to verify route_id/model_version present” checklist.

Connor O'Brien: I can own the “rollout state / known limitations” subsection as long as it’s not too deep.

Allison Grant: Perfect. I’ll add an “Owners” block at top w/ @mentions and a quarterly review reminder.

Aisha Bello: Can we schedule the first review for 2 weeks post-prod rollout? Things always shift after first customer ticket.

Sean Gallagher: Do it. Also add to oncall handoff checklist: “verify cache telemetry dashboards not broken”.

Allison Grant: Action items:
1) Allison: add first-5-min sanity checks + page vs ticket decision table + owners block.
2) Sergio: update example queries (rate/increase) + add cardinality-drop panel link.
3) Logan/Nadia: provide exact wording for cardinality guard + label allowlist references.
4) Omar: revise mitigation wording around cache bytes / memory pressure.
5) Ethan/Connor: add rollout/join-key caveats.

Allison Grant: Targeting EOD tomorrow for final publish.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: once those are in, consider it approved.

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: I’ll drop the label allowlist link + “what gets dropped” explanation in-thread.

Omar El-Sayed: :thumbsup: Will PR the mitigation wording and add note re: version_mismatch semantics.

Sergio Costa: I’ll update the panels + queries this afternoon and ping when ready.

Allison Grant: Thanks all. Closing the loop once edits are merged + runbook is marked “Production”.
