title: #eng-sre: 1740816603
preview:
Aisha Bello: hey folks — for the quantization profile beta canary, we need a few new dashboard panels ASAP. can someone confirm metric names + labels so we don’t invent new ones?

Aisha Bello: panels requested (per route + rollup):
- enabled routes (count + % of traffic)
- fallback rate (applied vs fallback vs disabled)
- error rate (5xx + policy/runtime errors)
- latency (p50/p95 decode + total)
- cost/token delta if we have it (nice to have)

Aisha Bello: also would like filters: `route_id`, `tenant_id` (or org), `model_id`, `region`, `deployment_tier`.

Logan Wright: :eyes: yep, we should align on names. what’s currently exported from runtime vs control plane? (don’t want 2 sources).

Caleb Johnson: runtime already emits request-level counters + histos w/ `quant_profile_id` annotation (post-18555). control plane has config state but not request truth.

Nadia Rahman: cost/token delta is in the cost attribution pipeline, but it’s lagging + sampled. better to show “tokens/sec + GPU util + avg prompt/decode tokens” as leading indicators; we can add cost later.

Aisha Bello: makes sense. for “enabled routes” i was thinking we can show % of traffic where profile is applied (not just config says enabled).

Caleb Johnson: +1. config toggle alone doesn’t mean applied (compat can fail). i’d recommend:
- `redwood_inference_requests_total{quant_resolution="applied|fallback|disabled"}`
- optionally break out fallback reason.

Allison Grant: jumping in — we should keep metric prefix consistent with other runtime panels. i think it’s `redwood.runtime.*` in OTEL -> Prom bridge? can we paste exact names?

Logan Wright: quick check from metrics catalog (latest):
- Counter: `redwood_runtime_requests_total`
  labels: `route_id`, `model_id`, `region`, `deployment_tier`, `status_code`, `quant_resolution`, `quant_profile_id` (empty if disabled)
- Counter: `redwood_runtime_quant_fallback_total`
  labels: above + `fallback_reason`
- Histogram: `redwood_runtime_request_latency_ms_bucket` (and _sum/_count)
  labels: above + `phase` (prefill|decode|total)

Logan Wright: does that match what you’re seeing, @Caleb?

Caleb Johnson: mostly. small correction: latency is split into two histos right now:
- `redwood_runtime_phase_latency_ms` (histo) w/ `phase=prefill|decode`
- `redwood_runtime_total_latency_ms` (histo) no phase label

Caleb Johnson: labels include `route_id`, `model_id`, `region`, `deployment_tier`, `quant_resolution`, `quant_profile_id`. we dropped `status_code` label on latency to reduce cardinality.

Allison Grant: thank you. please avoid `tenant_id` label on runtime metrics — that will explode. we can filter by tenant via route mapping (Console can show route->tenant), but dashboards should be route-centric.

Aisha Bello: ok, we’ll do route-centric. for “error rate”, can we use `redwood_runtime_requests_total` w/ `status_class` label? or just status_code?

Logan Wright: `status_code` is on the requests counter, yes. we also have `error_class` for internal failures:
- `redwood_runtime_errors_total{error_class="policy_incompatible|profile_fetch|kernel_unsupported|oom|timeout"}`

Chloe Martin: on control plane side, we can add a panel for “routes with config enabled” from `redwood_controlplane_route_quant_setting` gauge (0/1) if SRE wants to compare configured vs applied.

Aisha Bello: that comparison panel would be super useful (esp for incompat). what’s the gauge name + labels?

Ethan Park: gauge is `redwood_controlplane_route_quant_enabled` (0/1). labels: `route_id`, `deployment_tier`, `requested_profile_ref` (string), `resolved_profile_id` (string, can be empty), `compatibility="compatible|incompatible|unknown"`.

Ethan Park: (note: no region label here; region happens at routing/runtime).

Connor O'Brien: for alerts during canary, we’re planning:
- fallback_rate > 1% sustained 10m on canary routes
- error_rate (5xx) > baseline + 0.2% for 5m
- p95 total latency > baseline + 10% for 10m

Connor O'Brien: so pls include panels that make those easy to read + a quick “baseline vs quant” breakdown.

Aisha Bello: got it. metric names summary (tell me if wrong):
- applied/fallback breakdown: `redwood_runtime_requests_total` w/ `quant_resolution`
- fallback reasons: `redwood_runtime_quant_fallback_total` w/ `fallback_reason`
- errors: `redwood_runtime_errors_total`
- latency: `redwood_runtime_total_latency_ms` + `redwood_runtime_phase_latency_ms`
- config enabled: `redwood_controlplane_route_quant_enabled`

Aisha Bello: labels we’ll depend on: `route_id`, `model_id`, `region`, `deployment_tier`, `quant_profile_id`, `quant_resolution`, `fallback_reason`.

Caleb Johnson: yep. also `quant_profile_id` will be `"none"` not empty in some exporters — handle both.

Logan Wright: +1 and please don’t group by `quant_profile_id` by default (cardinality). make it a drilldown.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: also make sure fallback panel includes both “fallback” and “disabled” so it’s obvious when kill-switch is on.

Aisha Bello: perfect. i’ll update the dashboard JSON + add 3 panels:
1) Traffic by quant_resolution (stacked) + filter by route_id
2) Fallback rate + fallback_reason breakdown
3) Config enabled vs applied % (side-by-side)

Incident Bot: (info) Reminder: use folder `Inference / Quant Profiles` in Grafana and tag dashboards with `team:sre` and `feature:quant-profiles`.

Chloe Martin: when you have the dashboard link, drop it here — i can sanity check the control plane gauge in staging.

Connor O'Brien: pls aim EOD today so we can include in tomorrow’s canary checklist.

Aisha Bello: will do. thanks all.

title: #eng-sre: 1740786631
preview:
Sean Gallagher: seeing a dip on the cache hit-rate dashboard in hosted. question for oncall policy: do we page on this kind of hit-rate drop or just ticket it?

Sean Gallagher: screenshot-ish numbers (global hosted): prefix_hr down ~0.44 -> ~0.36 over ~25m. kv reuse also down a bit. no customer-reported issues yet.

Allison Grant: pls don’t page on hit-rate drop alone. we said guardrail only unless there’s evidence of regression (pressure/evictions/telemetry missing). does it line up w/ memory pressure?

Sean Gallagher: checking now. pressure gauge looks flat-ish, hovering ~0.62. evictions not spiking. wasted prefill ratio up though (0.08 -> 0.12).

Aisha Bello: wasted prefill can go up with traffic mix shifts too (more novel prompts / longer first turns). did prompt length buckets shift?

Sean Gallagher: yeah, prompt-length histogram shifted toward 4k-8k bucket in the last hour. also more traffic on /chat/stream route.

Allison Grant: that sounds like mix change. I’d ticket it + annotate dashboard (workload shift) but no page.

Sergio Costa: +1. if we page on this we’re going to get flappy pages every time a big tenant changes behavior.

Neha Kapoor: agree. paging criteria should require *platform signal* (pressure > threshold OR eviction rate up OR telemetry missing / ingestion drop). hit-rate dips by themselves are expected.

Sean Gallagher: ok but what about customer impact? lower hit rate => cost up, maybe latency up.

Aisha Bello: for paging, I’d rather key off latency SLO + error rate. cache is a lever; a hit-rate dip only pages if it’s likely a regression we can fix quickly.

Logan Wright: also want to differentiate “real dip” vs “metrics weird”. any sign of ingestion label drops / sample rate drop?

Sean Gallagher: telemetry sample rate looks normal. not a “flatline to zero” situation. so not telemetry-missing.

Allison Grant: then definitely ticket.

Connor O'Brien: fyi we did a small routing tweak earlier today to push more traffic to us-east for capacity reasons. could also be locality issues (less cache reuse across nodes).

Sean Gallagher: that’s interesting. could cause lower reuse without pressure. still: ticket, not page.

Sergio Costa: if we want to automate that logic: 
- PAGE only if (hr_now < hr_7d - 0.12) AND (kv_memory_pressure > 0.90 OR eviction_rate > 2x hr_7d) AND qps > X
- otherwise SEV4 ticket (triage)

Aisha Bello: and add “mix shift guardrail” in runbook: if prompt-length distribution changes by >N% (e.g. 1k+ bucket share up) treat as likely workload-driven.

Allison Grant: careful on “mix shift detector” in alerts, might be complicated. but we can put it in triage checklist.

Neha Kapoor: +1. keep alert rules simple, keep runbook smart.

Sean Gallagher: ok decision: no page for this event. I’ll open a ticket to look into routing/locality + confirm no regression.

Logan Wright: when you file the ticket, include miss-reason breakdown too. if version_mismatch/hash_mismatch spikes, it’s a rollout issue; if capacity/eviction spikes, it’s runtime/infra.

Sean Gallagher: will do.

Observability Bot: created Jira ticket https://redwood-inference.atlassian.net/browse/OBS-2147 (summary: "Hosted prefix_hr dip ~25m; likely workload mix shift / routing locality")

Allison Grant: thanks. can someone (Sergio?) add a short note to the alert docs: “hit-rate dip == ticket unless pressure/evictions/telemetry missing”.

Sergio Costa: yep, I’ll update the alert annotations in observability-pack + link to runbook section.

Aisha Bello: also suggest we add an annotation stream on the dashboard for routing changes / model rollouts so these are obvious.

Connor O'Brien: can do. we already emit deploy annotations; I can add routing config change annotations (at least for hosted).

Neha Kapoor: perfect. goal is fewer false pages, faster triage.

Sean Gallagher: closing loop: ticket filed, no page. will post update if it turns into pressure/evictions/latency.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup:

title: #eng-sre: 1740775807
preview:
Sergio Costa: Heads up: after the last couple burst beta exercises, the new burst alerts are *chatty*. We had ~14 pages in 24h, most looked like short-lived saturation / scrape gaps. Want to tune before we re-enable for the next beta tenants.

Sean Gallagher: yeah agree. goal is still: fast detection for neighbor impact + protect non-burst SLOs. But we can’t page on every 90s blip.

Allison Grant: I pulled the alert history. Biggest offenders:
- `BurstPoolSaturationHigh` firing on single-scrape spikes (pool_free_gpus dips to 0 for 1-2 samples)
- `BurstDenialRateHigh` in eu-west during telemetry lag (denial reasons flip to `TELEMETRY_STALE` for ~3-4m)
- label cardinality causing Grafana panel timeouts (per-tenant + per-model labels on the same alert)

Rafael Mendes: +1 on the cardinality. Also the page noise made oncall start ignoring the channel which is… not ideal.

Peter Holtz: from routing side, the “bad” events we actually care about looked like:
- non-burst tenants p95 jump + tail amplification
- admission controller latency / timeouts (routing waits, then falls back)
- circuit breaker opening too late
The saturation spikes by themselves weren’t harmful when the fallback policy did the right thing.

Keiko Yamamoto: infra perspective: pool telemetry is noisy at 1m windows. autoscaler + binpack reshuffles cause brief `free=0` but it’s not a real “no capacity” situation unless it persists.

Sergio Costa: proposal v1:
1) change `BurstPoolSaturationHigh` from `free_gpus == 0` to `pool_utilization > 0.98` AND `queue_depth > 0` for 10m.
2) demote that to ticket unless also `burst_admit_latency_p95 > 250ms`.

Sean Gallagher: ok. but we need explicit neighbor protection alert that stays paging.

Allison Grant: we have the neighbor signal in the metrics taxonomy draft: `dedicated_nonburst_p95_latency_delta` (computed vs rolling baseline per tenant route).
Right now the alert is:
- page if delta > 15% for 5m for *any* tenant
That’s why it’s paging constantly for small tenants.

Logan Wright: careful: that delta is super sensitive when baseline is low traffic. We need a min sample threshold or traffic gate, otherwise the stats are garbage.

Rafael Mendes: can we gate by request rate? e.g. only evaluate if `rps > 1` (or whatever) and `n_requests >= 500` in the window.

Allison Grant: yep. We can do `sum(rate(requests_total[5m])) by (tenant) > X` as a condition. Also we should move from “any tenant pages” to “top-N impacted tenants + pool-level signal”.

Peter Holtz: also could page on *aggregate* non-burst impact across the pool:
- `p95_latency_delta_nonburst_weighted` > 20% for 10m
and then have a dashboard panel showing per-tenant breakdown.

Sean Gallagher: I like: page on pool-level impact, ticket on per-tenant outliers.

Sergio Costa: ok so:
- Paging alert: `NonBurstNeighborImpactPool` if weighted p95 delta > 20% for 10m AND nonburst error rate doesn’t explain it (to avoid paging on unrelated deploy issues).
- Ticket alert: `NonBurstNeighborImpactTenant` if a specific tenant delta > 30% for 15m with traffic gate.

Keiko Yamamoto: thresholds: 20% for 10m seems reasonable. we saw real “bad” events were more like 35-50% sustained.

Allison Grant: +1. Also add a separate page for `tail_amp_ratio` (p99/p50) if it jumps. Tail amplification was the first symptom during the beta overload case.

Logan Wright: if we add tail amp, please don’t label it with tenant+model+route. Keep it at `{region, hw_class, pool}`. Otherwise we’re back to cardinality hell.

Sergio Costa: agreed. We’ll keep alert labels minimal:
- region
- hw_class
- pool
- severity
and link to a drilldown dashboard for tenant/model.

Rafael Mendes: what about `BurstDenialRateHigh`? it paged a bunch but the customer experience was just “more 429s” which is expected at cap.

Peter Holtz: denial rate alone isn’t enough. paging only if denial reason indicates a platform fault:
- `ADMISSION_TIMEOUT`
- `POLICY_EVAL_ERROR`
- `TELEMETRY_STALE` (if > 10m)
If it’s `TENANT_CAP_REACHED` or `POOL_CAP_REACHED`, that’s not a page; it’s either expected or an account config problem.

Allison Grant: we already have denial reason codes. We can do alert filtering with `reason=~"ADMISSION_TIMEOUT|POLICY_EVAL_ERROR"`.
For telemetry stale: page if `telemetry_lag_seconds_p95 > 120` for 10m (since that breaks determinism + fairness).

Sean Gallagher: +1. telemetry lag should be paging because it causes unfair denials + can hide real saturation.

Keiko Yamamoto: make sure we don’t page during planned metric pipeline deploys.

Logan Wright: we can add a maintenance silencer in the deploy pipeline; or better, use the existing `telemetry_pipeline_maintenance` flag as an inhibit.

Sergio Costa: action items:
- [Sergio] update Grafana alerts: add traffic gate + window changes + severity split (page vs ticket)
- [Allison] define the weighted pool-level nonburst p95 delta query + tail amp query (low-cardinality)
- [Logan] confirm min sample thresholds that won’t misfire for low-traffic tenants
- [Peter] confirm which denial reasons are considered platform faults vs expected caps

Sean Gallagher: also: align with rollout gate. If `NonBurstNeighborImpactPool` pages or burns > X error budget, we should auto-disable burst in that region/hw class.

Sergio Costa: yep, I’ll add an annotation on the paging alert pointing to the kill-switch + runbook section.

Rafael Mendes: can we test the new thresholds in replay mode before we flip? don’t want to find out oncall night.

Allison Grant: we can run them as “mute” alerts for 48h (record-only) and compare against incident timeline.

Sean Gallagher: do that. then we’ll re-review Friday and decide if we’re comfortable re-enabling eu-west.

Sergio Costa: sounds good. I’ll post PR link in this thread once it’s up.

Sean Gallagher: do that. then we’ll re-review Friday and decide if we’re comfortable re-enabling eu-west.

Sergio Costa: sounds good. I’ll post PR link in this thread once it’s up.

title: #eng-sre: 1740662244
preview:
Sean Gallagher: We need to lock observability requirements for retention purge jobs before ENG starts wiring more stores. What are the *must-have* metrics + alerts + runbook content? Also please call out label/cardinality landmines.

Allison Grant: +1. If we ship this without sane cardinality we’re gonna regret it. Let’s define:
- metrics names + labels
- dashboard panels
- alert thresholds (page vs ticket)
- runbook: how to re-run + how to confirm deletes + how to throttle

Logan Wright: From telemetry side, we can emit from purge job framework + per-store adapters. Current draft metric list:
- `retention_ttl_lag_seconds` (gauge)
- `retention_purge_rows_deleted_total` (counter)
- `retention_purge_failures_total` (counter)
- `retention_purge_backlog_rows` (gauge) OR backlog by oldest row age
- `retention_purge_job_duration_seconds` (histogram)
- `retention_purge_job_runs_total` (counter)

Caleb Johnson: For SRE we should model lag + failure rate + “stuckness”. Lag alone is ambiguous if the job is disabled.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. Biggest oncall pain is “purge silently not running” or “running but never catching up”. We need a hard pager on those.

Allison Grant: Cardinality: please do NOT label by `tenant_id`, `org_id`, `customer`, `project`, `route`, `model`, etc. If you need tenant-level debugging, do it via logs / trace IDs, not Prom labels.

Logan Wright: Yep. Proposed labels:
- `store` (enum: request_logs, audit_logs, traces, metrics, artifacts)
- `env` (prod/stage)
- `region` (us-east-1, eu-west-1, etc)
- `result` (success/failure/skipped)
- `tier` (hosted/dedicated/private)
- `reason` only on skip/fail, but kept very small set (validation, hold, dependency, throttled)

Caleb Johnson: +1. Also keep `job_name` out unless it’s a bounded enum. Framework sometimes generates per-tenant job names — we cannot export that.

Kevin Osei: On legal hold: purge job will “skip” deletions for holds. We’ll need a metric to confirm holds are preventing deletions without looking like failure.

Jordan Lee: The evaluator can return `effective_policy_state` for a store/tenant: enabled | disabled | hold. But again, we can’t label with tenant.

Allison Grant: Exactly. We can add a gauge aggregated across tenants via periodic aggregation job (low cardinality):
- `retention_policy_state_tenants` with labels `store`, `region`, `tier`, `state` and value = count of tenants.
This gives SRE a sanity check without cardinality blowup.

Sean Gallagher: Nice. That helps catch “oops everyone is in disabled logging mode” too.

Logan Wright: For `retention_ttl_lag_seconds`, what should it represent? We can compute as (now - oldest_row_past_ttl_timestamp) for each store/region, aggregated max.

Caleb Johnson: Make it explicit:
- `retention_ttl_lag_seconds{store,region,tier}` = max lag across partitions (or max of shard lags)
- plus maybe p95 lag if we can afford it, but max is what triggers paging.

Allison Grant: Be careful: max across all tenants could be noisy if one tenant has a huge backlog, but that’s still a real issue. I’m okay paging on max if we also have a safe threshold + some suppression.

Sean Gallagher: Let’s do:
Pager:
- lag > 6h for audit_logs or request_logs in prod for 30m
Ticket:
- lag > 1h for 2h (early warning)
Also we should tune per store. Audit logs are the one customers will ask about.

Kevin Osei: Audit logs exporter uses windows (e.g., last 90 days). If purge lags by days, evidence exports might contain too much.

Allison Grant: For paging thresholds: I’d prefer:
- Request logs: page if `lag > 12h` for 1h
- Audit logs: page if `lag > 6h` for 30m
- Traces/metrics: ticket if `lag > 24h` (unless we have an SLO)
But if product wants a tighter SLO, we can adjust.

Sean Gallagher: Also page on job failure rate:
- `increase(retention_purge_failures_total[15m]) / increase(retention_purge_job_runs_total[15m]) > 0.2` for 30m (per store/region)
Or simpler: consecutive failures.

Caleb Johnson: Consecutive failure alert is much more readable.
- `retention_purge_last_success_timestamp` gauge (seconds since epoch)
- page if `time() - last_success > 1h` for audit_logs/request_logs
This avoids weirdness when runs are low volume.

Logan Wright: We can emit `retention_purge_last_success_timestamp` from the scheduler per store/region.

Allison Grant: Add also `retention_purge_last_run_timestamp` so we can differentiate “scheduler dead” vs “job runs but never succeeds”.

Sean Gallagher: Dashboard panels I want:
1) TTL Lag (by store, region)
2) Purge throughput (rows/sec) and rows deleted
3) Purge duration p50/p95
4) Failure counts + last success age
5) Backlog size / backlog age
6) Policy state tenant counts (enabled/disabled/hold)

Caleb Johnson: + panel for “DB pressure guardrail” (if applicable): if purge uses batch deletes with a safety limit, we should visualize “throttled” runs.

Logan Wright: We have a throttle mode when DB load is high. We can tag result=skipped, reason=throttled and emit `retention_purge_throttle_events_total`.

Allison Grant: `reason` label is OK if the set is fixed and small. Please document the allowed values in the runbook and enforce in code.

Sean Gallagher: Paging policy / oncall flow:
- Pager triggers for prod audit_logs/request_logs only (for now)
- Everything else to ticket in #eng-sre + Jira SRE queue
- Include runbook link in alert annotations

Kevin Osei: Runbook needs “verification” steps too. We keep getting asked “how do we prove purge happened”. We should standardize:
- check `retention_ttl_lag_seconds` trending down
- check `rows_deleted_total` increments
- spot-check DB count query (internal only)
- check sample tenant via secure admin query tool

Allison Grant: Yes, but runbook must be safe/limited. Anything “query per tenant” should be break-glass and audited.

Sean Gallagher: Put it as: “If customer escalation / compliance incident: follow break-glass procedure, link INT ticket template.”

Caleb Johnson: Also please add a section: “What NOT to do” (don’t bump batch size unbounded, don’t disable hold checks, don’t restart without checking backlog).

Logan Wright: One more note: metrics scrape interval is 60s in prod. Histograms can be heavy. For `job_duration_seconds`, do we need histogram or just summary gauge?

Allison Grant: Histogram is fine but keep buckets tight and limited. Most jobs should be seconds-minutes. Proposed buckets: 1s, 5s, 15s, 30s, 60s, 120s, 300s, 600s.

Sean Gallagher: Works. Also “rows deleted per run” is useful for seeing if we’re hitting safety cap.

Logan Wright: We can emit `retention_purge_rows_deleted_per_run` gauge at end of run, labeled store/region/tier.

Allison Grant: That’s okay (bounded labels). But don’t make it per shard.

Jordan Lee: For policy drift: we planned a separate dashboard panel “tenants with policy drift vs defaults”. That requires tenant dimension though.

Allison Grant: For now keep drift as *counts* by tier/region. If you need tenant list, generate a daily report artifact stored internally (not Prom). SRE can use that for targeted follow-up.

Sean Gallagher: Cool. Action items:
- Logan/Caleb: confirm final metric names + labels and post here for signoff
- Allison: draft alert rules (pager vs ticket) with thresholds above
- Kevin: add runbook section for verification + break-glass steps

Grafana Bot: Alert preview (staging): `retention_ttl_lag_seconds{store="audit_logs",region="us-east-1"} > 21600` firing for 0m (rule not yet enabled)

Allison Grant: lol staging already yelling at us :)

Sean Gallagher: ok but that’s why we do this. Please keep staging rules non-paging.

Logan Wright: Will post concrete metric contract later today. Also will add label whitelist enforcement in framework so we can’t accidentally add `tenant_id`.

Caleb Johnson: +1 on label whitelist. Make it a unit test. We’ve been burned.

Sean Gallagher: Thanks all. Let’s treat this as the contract for ENG-18452 dashboards/alerts + the purge framework PR hooks.

title: #eng-sre: 1740499033
preview:
Allison Grant: seeing a bunch of questions in the last few days: “hit-rate dipped” vs “metrics stopped flowing”. we need a clear way to detect ingestion drops for the new cache telemetry before we start paging ppl on fake regressions

Aisha Bello: +1. right now dashboards show a clean cliff to 0 and everyone assumes regression. but for cache, 0 can mean either “no hits” or “we’re missing the signal”

Sean Gallagher: the worst case is we page oncall for “cache efficiency SLO burn” and it’s just pipeline / label-drop

Logan Wright: from telemetry side we have a couple failure modes:
- prometheus remote_write backlog / drop
- ingestion cardinality guard drops (label not allowed -> series dropped)
- exporter disabled via flag (env config)
- runtime emitting but gateway tags missing -> metrics arrive but unsliceable (looks like “all unknown”)

Nadia Rahman: we should explicitly model a “data completeness” signal. otherwise hit-rate alone isn’t safe to alarm on

Caleb Johnson: +1. the cardinality guard drop is the sneaky one. it won’t show as “missing scrape”, you just get fewer series / missing labels

Thomas Baird: can we define what “telemetry missing” means operationally? something like: for each env/region/model_family we expect some baseline QPS and therefore some cache_* counters

Allison Grant: yeah, goal is “how do we distinguish real cache behavior change from telemetry missing?”

Sergio Costa: suggestion: add an alert on metric freshness/absence for the *raw counters* used to compute hit-rate. prom has `absent()` and `increase()` checks

Aisha Bello: example? for cache hit-rate we use `redwood_cache_kv_hits_total` and `_misses_total` (names TBD but you get it)

Sergio Costa: something like:
- `absent(sum by (region, deployment_mode) (rate(redwood_cache_kv_requests_total[5m])))` (or the actual emitted “requests_total”) 
- or “rate == 0” but only when gateway request rate is non-zero

Logan Wright: we do have gateway-side request counters that are very reliable. easiest join: if `gateway_requests_total` is steady but runtime cache counters disappear, it’s telemetry

Nadia Rahman: careful: runtime cache metrics are sampled. so “missing” has to account for sampling. but “completely absent for 10m in a region w/ traffic” is still a red flag

Allison Grant: so logic:
1) is there traffic? (gateway QPS > threshold)
2) are cache counters present and increasing (even at sampled rate)?
3) if not, treat as telemetry missing and suppress cache hit-rate regression alerts

Sean Gallagher: +1. and for the dashboards we should show “data freshness” banner. people need to see “warning: cache telemetry incomplete”

Caleb Johnson: we can also expose ingestion-side stats: `telemetry_drop_total{reason="cardinality_guard"}` and `telemetry_series_dropped_total` per metric namespace. if those spike, it’s not a runtime regression

Thomas Baird: +1 we already have those counters in the ingestion service, but they’re not wired to the cache dashboards

Allison Grant: can we add a panel in the cache dashboard: “Ingestion drops (cache metrics)” + “remote_write lag” + “series count by metric”?

Logan Wright: yes. we can tag ingestion drop metrics by `metric_prefix="redwood_cache_"` to avoid per-metric cardinality

Aisha Bello: another failure mode: dashboards show “0 hit rate” because denominator is zero and query does `hits/(hits+misses)` -> NaN -> gets rendered as 0 in some panels. we should fix queries to show null / N/A

Sergio Costa: yep. prom query should do something like:
```
(sum(rate(hits[5m])) / clamp_min(sum(rate(hits[5m]) + rate(misses[5m])), 1))
```
BUT that still yields small numbers when missing. better: compute ratio only when denominator present, otherwise return NaN and show as “no data”

Nadia Rahman: agree. also: use `or` to preserve NaN behavior carefully. we can standardize query templates

Sean Gallagher: re alerting: I don’t want to page on “telemetry missing” unless it’s prolonged. ticket first, page only if it impacts multiple products or SLO reporting

Allison Grant: so:
- SEV/page: only if cache telemetry missing >30m in prod OR multiple regions OR remote_write down
- otherwise: create ticket to telemetry/oncall channel

Connor O'Brien: from release side: telemetry missing can also be caused by a bad rollout (flag off in new runtime build). could we catch that with a canary?

Thomas Baird: synthetic canary request is good. like one job per region that sends a known prompt twice (guaranteed prefix reuse) and checks we see both a miss then a hit. this verifies end-to-end + semantics

Logan Wright: +1. we can emit a “cache_canary_success” gauge from the canary job into our normal metrics. then dashboard/alert: canary failing != hit-rate regression

Aisha Bello: but we should ensure canary doesn’t log prompts. just a short static string and no customer context. and mark it with `tenant="internal"` / `route="cache_canary"` (allowlisted)

Caleb Johnson: careful on labels: if we introduce canary route label, it must be in allowlist, otherwise it will get dropped and we’ll chase ghosts again

Nadia Rahman: I’ll add `route="cache_canary"` to the allowlist doc + ingestion rules

Allison Grant: what about the “label drop” case where metrics still exist but route/model tags missing so the dashboard slice shows nothing?

Logan Wright: that’s a joinability issue. we can detect via an “unknown/other” bucket. enforce: if `route_id` missing, map to `route="unknown"` not drop. then we can alert on %unknown rising

Sergio Costa: yes! add a panel: “% metrics with route=unknown / model_version=unknown”. if it spikes, tagging broke

Aisha Bello: and that’s how we separate: “real cache regression” would still show tagged metrics, just worse ratios; “telemetry missing/tagging broken” shows unknown bucket spike or absent series

Sean Gallagher: ok action items?

Allison Grant: proposing:
1) Dashboard: add top-row “Telemetry health” panels:
   - gateway QPS (baseline)
   - cache metrics presence (rate of `cache_events_total` or hits+misses)
   - ingestion drops for `redwood_cache_*` (cardinality guard + other)
   - %unknown labels
2) Alerts:
   - `CacheTelemetryMissing` (ticket): gateway QPS>threshold AND cache counters absent for 10m
   - `CacheTelemetryMissingSev` (page): same condition for 30m OR >=2 regions
   - `CacheTelemetryLabelDrop` (ticket): %unknown >X% for 15m
3) Query hygiene: ratio panels should show N/A when denominator absent, not 0
4) Canaries: implement cache canary job per region (Connor/Thomas)

Logan Wright: sounds good. I’ll take (1) ingestion drop metrics + tagging. Caleb can help w/ guard counters

Caleb Johnson: yep, I’ll expose `dropped_series_total{metric_prefix="redwood_cache_"}` and `dropped_samples_total{reason=...}`

Sergio Costa: I can do the prom/alert rules + dashboard panels PR

Nadia Rahman: I’ll update the metrics schema/allowlist for canary route + unknown bucket behavior

Connor O'Brien: I’ll coordinate canary rollout w/ release, make sure it runs in staging + canary + prod

Sean Gallagher: last point: suppression. if `CacheTelemetryMissing` firing, we should automatically inhibit “cache hit-rate regression” alerts (so oncall doesn’t get both)

Allison Grant: +1. Sergio can you add alertmanager inhibition rule?

Sergio Costa: yep. `inhibit: CacheHitRateRegression if CacheTelemetryMissing` same region/deployment_mode labels

MetricsBot: Reminder set by Allison Grant: “Review cache telemetry drop detection panels + alert rules” in 3 days.

Aisha Bello: thanks. once this is in, we can be way more confident that a hit-rate dip is real (workload/policy) vs missing pipes

Allison Grant: cool. pls link PRs here when up
